My brain is bad..just very bad. I'm always tired, and have been for years. Parts of my memory feel decent...people are constantly astounded that I remember their buying preferences at my job at Walgreens as a Cashier, but I don't remember my childhood very well at all except for weird things like my dad letting me try a very tiny disgusting sip of Budweiser between ages 4-6. I remember a few other things, but not very much at all...but maybe thats normal. I fluctuate between 270-280 and keep going back to eating junk after only 3-4 days at most of trying to eat well. Changing my brain feels impossible...I wish I could afford better food and could stop my brain from spazzing every time I try to deny myself carbohydrates...I've read constantly about being able to get over carb cravings, but can't think of how to do this when I can't even get beyond a week eating low amounts. I felt callous and bad for it. I didn't even consider it for a second...not sure why I'm going in to all this. I guess just for background...so I can hear what others think about it..though I'm afraid of the response..

Sorry if this is annoying to read or if I'm annoying because I keep saying sorry..anyway I guess this'll do for now...not sure what I expect..but I hope something can help me. Some advice some..Anyway I guess I'm done for now. Thanks for any thoughts or ideas anyone can give me.

I want to have a wife someday..maybe even kids and I want to be healthy. I want to get a better job than Walgreens. My self esteem issues and my health in combination with everything is holding me to a life where I feel like screaming..there are people with far worse lives than me that handle them better..I just don't understand how..how to change how to be a better person....how to not feel pathetic...Anyway before I write for days I'll stop for now...really this time...

### [email redacted] — 2014-11-19T22:11:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-thermogenesis-temps-how-long.12190/post-147850>

Jonathan. First off I find you very honest and very un-pretentious. It takes real courage to lay out your problems as you do. About the whole subject of "Cold" and let me be very clear here I am speaking ONLY for myself but I can relate to what you are saying very much. Personally I CANNOT do it unless in the presence of 'warmth'!!! What I mean is I do it at the beach here in LA only after a good dose of sunshine and I go in but only as long as it does not begin to feel 'masochistic'. Now this can vary also according to circumstance like I have notices if I am with someone talking etc in the water I can and tend to say MUCH LONGER than I do by myself. By myself it can easily start to feel 'masochistic' and not actually good for me.

I know I am getting into the area of 'feelings' here and Dr Kruse is more concerned with the biology and all the rest of it. But the way he describes the "Fournier Effect" well that seems to work for me heat alternating with cold. Also I find if I do it that way and never 'force' myself to do, do it as it feels good and as I WANT even to do it I find some really amazing 'mental effects' it literally works like a 'shrink' as is used in psycho-therapy but a better shrink than I have found up to now!! It the 'shrink' of the ocean itself and the cold in it. It's like I can 'get under' a lot of my resentments mental blocks of all kinds. I don't feel this happening at all if I 'force' it like have an ice bath in the morning or something.

Dr Kruse says a lot cold is not 'hormetic' I think that means it causes stress. But I wonder COULD it or CAN it cause stress. I suspect it can and I think it is worth thinking about this.................cold is not just cold there is a context and lots of other factors. Also I am tempted to borrow Dr Kruse's statement like maybe cold can open the doors of Heaven or Hell depending......................I think there is a danger of 'masochism' and inducing stress and yet it can be the complete opposite. Also I know something about the dangers of 'masochism' I was brought up in a very strict Irish Catholic family and for years I was involved in what is called "Primal Therapy" which while again can and sometimes is a good idea also goes over into 'masochism' and become counter-productive. I think as biological creatures we are hard wired to seek pleasure and what feels good, we recoil from pain and if the cold feels 'painful' we recoil from it and I think it is quite possible that it is 'stressful'

Also I am aware there are other issues...............for example I pretty much know I do not have nearly enough DHA in my membranes which I think makes in much harder to tolerate cold. After all isn't it the presence of lots of DHA which allows fish to live (and see) in some REALLY cold environments. So for all these reasons Jonathan my 'advice' for what it's worth is go easy on yourself a bit you may be trying to do too much too quickly and there is then a danger of getting disillusioned. I hope you find this somewhat helpful

### Jack Kruse — 2014-11-20T03:27:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-9-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12199/post-147882>

Pat T2D is a disease of blue light.........you cant run from it in LA.

Light-sensitive proteins, like FAD which you just learned about in this blog, absorb light strongly at certain frequencies and become excited to send out an electric signal or to re-emit light. Well in cytochrome one where FAD is.........when you have too much blue light coming in.........you expand the atoms in this protein changing the waves it makes. Those wave change the tuning of your inner mitochondria membrane..........so you can't use electrons from certain long light foods like carbs like the rest of us can. When you cant you can not make superoxide. When you can't make that.........sleep is trashed and your mitochondria become like a row boat with one oar.........the more you use just one oar the more you go in circles; in other words you can never recycle your mitochondria to break your cycle.......and T2D is your result.........this is why you need both oars to get to your goal of optimal and away from T2D. LA enslaves you to this problem.......and it operate below your perception level........ As this thread clearly shows. You are actually teaching people and I applaud this. But I want you to get the message!!!!
For example, there are three kinds of rhodopsins, light-sensitive proteins in the eye that absorb at red, blue and green respectively. The bioluminescent protein aequorin absorbs a similar wavelength of light as the rhodopsin that absorbs blue light. In the consensus spectrum of blue rhodopsins and aequorins, there is only one prominent peak frequency at 0.475, and this is most likely to be the one related to the absorption of blue light.

Blue light speeds up DNA and RNA expression when it is not monitored by both oars:
The effects of their frequencies of electromagnetic radiation have been shown directly on cell growth and DNA synthesis by
Veljko Veljkovic and Irina Cosic. Blue light were shown in experiment to indeed be maximal at the predicted wavelengths that fostered excessive cell growth by their actions nucleic acids.

Blue light.........NOT FOOD. LA =BLUE LIGHT This is the gasoline for the fire that began in the egg of your grandmother..........before you breathed your first breath because of choices they made to live outside your biologic design.

What it means is that a protein or DNA sequence tied to this blue light oscillation or wave (like the FAD protein is tied to the insulin protein) generally has more than one function. FAD and insulin are your oars in this analogy. One without the other makes your mitochondria a fog chasing its tail chronically. FAD sings or vibrates/oscillates one note for each function it has to which its binding partner or other oar resonates (could be another protein like osteocalcin,or vitamin K2 in the case of insulin)

When you get this........you begin to see why supplementation is a money losing idea........

You have to coordinate the orchestra to make the music of life..........and the conductor is the circadian signals that mitochondria sense........and LA is doing you in.

Improperly paired and unpaired electrons of our ancestors may have very real consequences for us. If our mothers and grandmothers could not sense their environments=leptin resistance, it increases our own tendency toward being insensitive to the environment and a poor epigenetic prognosis. It is bad enough to be in a good environment when you can not sense it reliably. A bad environment is fairly certain to make a real mess of us when we cannot sense it well. It is not enough to look at how long our ancestors may have lived or how strong they were. The unique conditions around conception and in utero are more important. My mother had most of her thyroid removed at 4 and died of cancer at 64. She was born when her mother, a doctor, was 38 and had already mis-carried 4 times. I doubt I started strong shall we say.

### Josh — 2014-11-20T04:06:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-9-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12199/post-147890>

Roughly scaling some of this out into the Electric Universe, I imagine that inheriting poorly paired and unpaired electrons that have too much or the wrong charge would predispose us to the type of electrical events microcosmically that altered and shaped the solar system macrocosmically before the electrical charges of the various bodies floating around had stabilized/discharged=Birekland Current versus Field Free Current. The more stable our cellular environments are at conception, the more stable the electrical expression of the phenotype. Without the sensing of the environment and adjustments, we either build up too much charge leading to damaging electrical interactions between cells/organelles/molecules/atoms or not enough charge so that we leave our dead star mitochondria in place dooming our solar system to an early demise from lack of IR and such.

I imagine it as the difference between laminar and chaotic flow. Moderate electric charge on things=moderate electrical activity and regular behavior=pretty easy to understand if a log floating down river will hit your boat. At a threshold, this changes to a chaotic expression where the charge differentials related to the spin and pairing of electrons becomes more of a probabilistic situation where it is less possible to predict and control whether the log floating downstream is going to hit your boat.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-11-20T06:29:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-9-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12199/post-147907>

Szent-Györgi noted that the sites of primary energy transduction, the mitochondrial and chloroplast membranes, are closely analogous to the pn junction, a semiconductor device that facilitates the separation of positive and negative charges, and is capable of generating an electric current when excited by energy in the **_form of heat and/or light.
_
The answers are in this blog.**

### Jack Kruse — 2014-11-20T06:31:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-9-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12199/post-147908>

In common with these semi-conductor devices, various biological membranes and artificially constituted phospholipid membranes also exhibit thermoelectric, photoelectric and piezoelectric effects due respectively to heat (IR), light and mechanical pressure (thermodynamic changes of the tensional integrity of the cell).

### yewwei.tan — 2014-11-20T06:33:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-9-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12199/post-147909>

Just finished reading the blog post.

Random note about these 2 sentences which were just casually mentioned in the blog:

Cell membranes connect to the outer mitochondrial membrane by actin filaments that have integrins connected to them.

Integrins are designs to tighten or loosen the system to alter size and shape of everything in a cell.
​

I thought back to this post in the '50 grams of protein, no can do' thread -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/50-grams-or-protein-no-can-do.11940/#post-147768](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/50-grams-or-protein-no-can-do.11940/#post-147768)

Leucine is highly insulinogenic so that means it alter cell membrane oscillations and vibrations............(bread crumb)​

Paper '**Integrin αvβ3 acts downstream of insulin in normalization of interstitial fluid pressure in sepsis and in cell-mediated collagen gel contraction**' -- [http://ajpheart.physiology.org/content/295/2/H555#sec-9](http://ajpheart.physiology.org/content/295/2/H555#sec-9)

Insulin induced αvβ3-integrin-dependent collagen gel contraction mediated by C2C12 cell

THE INTERSTITIAL FLUID PRESSURE (PIF) is involved in control of the fluid flux across the capillary wall

It has been suggested that connective tissue cells actively control PIF by exerting a tension on the collagen/microfibrillar network of the tissue, thereby restraining the tissue and preventing the hyaluronan and proteoglycan ground substance of the connective tissue from taking up fluid and swelling
​

If you look at some of the figures of the results, the contraction is very significant. Example -- [http://ajpheart.physiology.org/content/ajpheart/295/2/H555/F1.medium.gif](http://ajpheart.physiology.org/content/ajpheart/295/2/H555/F1.medium.gif)

Contraction to 20% of the original surface area, without any semblance of a cell membrane, and at the high temperature of 37C. The figure also shows the importance of integrin, since inhibiting it basically leads to only a contraction to 90% of the original surface area. This difference is pretty huge!

More quotes from the paper:

Together, these findings suggest that insulin and PDGF-BB elicit a common PI3K-dependent signaling pathway that promotes cellular contraction and that is of importance in the control of PIF.

Insulin signaling is mediated mainly through the insulin receptor although insulin also binds and stimulates the structurally similar IGF-R1, albeit with substantially lower affinity, as well as the IGF-R1/insulin receptor hybrid receptors ([18](http://ajpheart.physiology.org/content/295/2/H555#ref-18), [21](http://ajpheart.physiology.org/content/295/2/H555#ref-21), [25](http://ajpheart.physiology.org/content/295/2/H555#ref-25)). IGF-1 that binds and activates IGF-R1 stimulates fibroblast-mediated collagen gel contraction ([12](http://ajpheart.physiology.org/content/295/2/H555#ref-12), [14](http://ajpheart.physiology.org/content/295/2/H555#ref-14)). It is currently not known whether insulin exerts its effects on PIF through the stimulation of the insulin receptors or IGF-R1.

We and others have previously shown that integrin αvβ3 can mediate collagen gel contraction but only when β1-integrins are absent or perturbed, and the results presented here are consistent with that ([5](http://ajpheart.physiology.org/content/295/2/H555#ref-5), [8](http://ajpheart.physiology.org/content/295/2/H555#ref-8), [15](http://ajpheart.physiology.org/content/295/2/H555#ref-15)).
​

As usual, not much of the actual mechanism to how insulin causes these effects on a magnetic or electric level is discussed, and I am not skilled enough to discern that from a quick reading of the paper.

\---

Referencing another paper about actin and insulin -- [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/9210235](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/9210235) . It seems like actin is responsible for transmitting the insulin receptor signal. I'm going to park my reading of the mechanism to focus on other aspects first.

\---

I have to think of the case of physiological insulin resistance, like it would naturally be when eating a high-fat in winter time. ie: Insulin is kept higher in plasma.

Now, does this mean that cells are being kept more condensed? Or does "insulin resistance" also mean that the downstream effects on integrin are diminished?

To answer that, I need to understand the mechanics of integrin and actin adhesion to the cell membrane, and the effects that magnetism and electricity will have on that. Oh well, looks like I got more reading ahead of me

...

### Jack Kruse — 2014-11-20T06:42:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-9-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12199/post-147914>

Many microorganisms move by means of a single flagellum. That of bacteria such as Salmonella can rotate up to 100 times a second, being driven by a molecular motor attached to its base and embedded in the cell membrane. The motor is entirely powered by the flow of protons (proticity) from one side of the cell membrane to the other.
WHY?

The best educators are those who show you where to look, but don’t tell you what to see.

Genes are quantum computers and the environment they exist in is their measuring devices. Observation effects their expression. This implies the environment your chronically expose them too..........directly affects the life they spit out for you. You seem blind to this simple concept. I am not sure why. You have to figure that out for you. No one can do it for you, even though you seem to want that specifically.
Failure is never the problem in life. The response to it is. We need to learn to fail forward to create success. This idea is built into DNA. Failure is the will to begin again, while apply intelligent design in the things you have learned. **Full effort is full victory in failure.** Stop focusing on risks; they multiply in your mind and paralyze your attention. When this happens you start to give control to others that might control your life. You should never do this.
You want to focus on the task, instead, on doing what needs to be done. Today, take responsibility and blame yourself; only you have the power to change and transform things in your life. If you assign responsibility elsewhere, then you are giving power to decide your fate outside your control.

Commitment and risk are two sides of the same coin. The more you commit, the lower the risk.

Convictions are greater enemies of truth than lies. Edit your life frequently and ruthlessly. It's your creation and masterpiece and not mine.

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=147915)
>
> enzymes have huge dipole moments..........WHY?

Read a bunch of stuff over 2 hours, haven't had sleep to consolidate any of that yet, so I still don't have any answers.

Still, heres' some random stray thoughts before bed, without citing any of the sources I've bee nreading. Probably will consolidate the information tomorrow and write down more thoughts:

-   Likelihood of Proton tunnelling definitely increases the more imbalanced the charge distribution is between any potential bonds between the Hydrogen atom. Free radicals obviously stand out as being very easily biased to have even more imbalanced charges.
-   Enzymatic proton wavepackets encode various energetically defined eigenstates. More matching eigenstates seems to correlate with higher chance of proton tunnelling. Eigenstate distribute seems to be dependent on geometry of the enzyme active site.
-   Looking at the geometry of F-Actin gives my a feeling that it can conform to the wave function of a more highly-charged protein. It looks like a very flat series of very polar segments, and is heavily dependent on chaperone and prefoldin proteins for it's final folded shape (which scientists apparent can't seem to find a canonical form -- maybe there isn't any, and the folding structure of actin networks is dependent on adjacent polar molecules)
-   Still don't understand the notion of critical energetic points and their implications on tunnelling probabilities
-   Adenine\* (proton tunelled adenine) combining with Cytosine instead of Thymime is a major brainfuck ..... Proton tunnelling due to electromagnetic forces can alter your genetics forever! Puts the idea of electrically and magnetically mediated "oocyte selection" into a whole new light.

10:30pm here. Good night.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-11-20T14:40:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-9-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12199/post-147957>

To create answers for your life you must be able to create entangled particles.........and I said quite clearly in this blog some are not capable of this. This means they live their life like a standard computer not a quantum computer.

When standard computers first appeared, most of their designers intended them for nothing only to do huge, mindless computations. That's why the
things were called "computers". Yet even then, a few pioneers --especially Alan Turing -- envisioned what's now called "Artificial
Intelligence" - or "AI". AI is a synthetic biologic version of what humans are capable of. A regular computer resembles what a sick or ill human is capable of doing as an analogy. They saw that computers might possibly go beyond arithmetic and mathematics and begin to create and think for themselves instead of having to require other humans to put data in for them to get the answers they seek. Moreover, maybe imitate the processes that go on inside human brains that are working well.

Today, with robots everywhere in industry and movie films, most people think Al has gone much further than it has. Yet still, "computer experts"
say machines will never really think. **_If so, how could they be so smart,and yet so dumb?
_**
We naturally admire our Einstein's and Beethoven's, and wonder if computers ever could create such wondrous theories or symphonies. Most
people think that creativity requires some special, magical "gift" that simply cannot be explained by midern science. I am explaining to you how this happens in the creation of entangled electrons and protons in you. The more you get the better thinking you create. Right now you have a deficit in that ability. If I am correct, then no computer or sick human brain will ill working mitochondria can really create solutions for what ails them because they cant see the problem because they cant see the scale of the problem as Prana expresses. Remember anything modern machines can do can be explained to most people. What I am discussing here this may not be true for all because of how they think.

To see what's wrong with that, we must avoid one naive trap. We mustn't only look at works our culture views as very great, until we first get good
ideas about how ordinary people do ordinary things. We can't expect to guess, right off, how great composers write great symphonies. I don't
believe that there's much difference between ordinary thought and highly creative thought. I don't blame anyone for not being able to do
everything the most creative people do. **_I don't blame them for not being able to explain it, either. I do object to the idea that, just because we can't
explain it now, then no one ever could imagine how creativity works._**

We shouldn't intimidate ourselves by our admiration of our Beethoven's and Einstein's. Instead, we ought to be annoyed by our ignorance of how
we get ideas - and not just our "creative" ones. Were so accustomed to the marvels of the unusual that we forget how little we know about the
marvels of ordinary thinking. Ordinary thinking is the domain of altered mitochondria...........this site is about the "extra" in extraordinary and how it is innovated within us.

Dr Janov was revered and followed until he wasn't......................and to flat out say to someone who is trying just to understand that they have some 'inability to entangle electrons' well maybe so but also maybe that's the beginning of Fascism and a kind of 'cult' thinking.

I probably will regret saying this but I start to find the atmosphere a bit suffocating, too many groupies, not enough questioning maybe some of you guys are not 'entangling electrons' so well lol. I am not some newbie here either I have been reading and following Dr Kruse from 2011 already. I am not some idiot up to now I have appreciated and tried to understand everything he has to say. My friends mock me they say instead of "Dr Kruse" I should just say "My Guru". So no need for people to patronize me like I am some baby who don't know how to walk or something. Maybe I don't but do all the people here?.

I understand the body is 'complicated' but there seems to be more and more 'obfuscation' quantum entanglement is not even 'proved' yet and now it has all these biological effects. Does is really or do you REALLY know? I am asking some fairly simple questions I DO NOT expect to be told what to do, tell me if you can and I can make up my own mind. No need for high handed jeremiads and way above the clouds advice. Frankly I find it unhelpful and VERY annoying

### Jack Kruse — 2014-11-20T18:38:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-9-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12199/post-147981>

Patrick your feelings are what they are. I can't change that. You chimmed in and said youre were confused and I tried to help you understand and you're still are pissed. I can't solve your feelings only you can. I can only show you what the science says you might consider. That choice is 100% yours and not that of a cult.

### [email redacted] — 2014-11-20T19:11:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-9-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12199/post-147983>

Dr Kruse: I am sure you have a good point. The thing is I was diagnosed as T2D 11 years ago and I have tried my best including try to implement EVERYTHING (magnetico, CT, DHA, no cell/wifi/blue light on and on and on) you say...............well maybe except not living in LA. But I asked or thought I did is there an 'aggregate effect' of EMF and I suppose I know the answer I am sure is is 'yes' But would it hurt to try to explain that a little bit and in English please. I feel 'played with' and taunted and not in a nice way.

I am 62 years old and have been around a bit, a long association with "Primal Therapy" and honestly I feel I can sense the beginnings of 'cult like' thinking. And to just say 'the science' says so not you THAT is even a kind of cult like way of talking. Because if the science says there is no arguing!! Just accept it or else be considered 'stupid' or something. I understand your frustrations with 'lessor minds' and honestly sometimes I wonder why you bother even with all of these 'bio-hackers' like I see you as streets ahead of them. Still.............and I 'admit' I am not in a good place I have to face the fact I don't seem to be able to 'conquer' T2D and maybe yes moving away from LA is the part of the puzzle for me to do now. I accept that I suppose. But even all that said the world is in very bad shape anyway, I heard today we may get 6 degrees Celsius warming by 2050. Is the world doomed anyway and for all the hope and need for DHA what if all the oceans are dead by that date. Where does that leave us, I think some 'humility' in the face of these facts would be more appropriate (better tunneling of electrons lol - see 2 can play that game) as a response To pull stuff like that at least to pull it routinely when anyone disagrees or simple don't understand IS 'cult like' IMO

Also IMHO to 'reduce' everything to protons, electrons, magnetism is 'reductionism' almost by definition. I think you miss the aspect of 'feeling' which is what the body is instinctively telling itself for example if Jonathan has a real hard time going in the pool in the morning I would say maybe listen to his body and what it is telling him rather than 'lectures' about 'mass equivalence' or whatever. I don't think that is helpful and where are all the 'masochists' who were doing extreme CT last winter......................are they STILL doing it I doubt it though I could be wrong...........to me there is a serious need for more 'humility' and more 'realism'. Misty Williams was all gung ho on heavy CT is she STILL..................does anyone know or care..................

### sjoshua — 2014-11-20T20:23:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-9-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12199/post-147991>

> [[email redacted] said:](/goto/post?id=147983)
>
> Dr Kruse: I am sure you have a good point. The thing is I was diagnosed as T2D 11 years ago and I have tried my best including try to implement EVERYTHING (magnetico, CT, DHA, no cell/wifi/blue light on and on and on) you say...............well maybe except not living in LA. But I asked or thought I did is there an 'aggregate effect' of EMF and I suppose I know the answer I am sure is is 'yes' But would it hurt to try to explain that a little bit and in English please. I feel 'played with' and taunted and not in a nice way.

Hey Pat, let's take the analogy of a microwave oven, as follows:
\- The inside of the microwave represents your environment
\- The 'power level' of the microwave is determined by the number and intensity of the sources of (non-native and native) EMF in your environment: wifi, cell towers, artificial light, the moon's phase/distance, your distance from the earth, etc.

Now, consider that you are a potato

When you cook a potato in a microwave, the higher you set the 'power level', the quicker the potato is going to cook. Think of the 'cooking' from the non-native EMF to be equivalent to causing chaos at the cellular level - be it cancer, T2D, or whatever other disease each specific fleshpod/(human body) has as it's weakest link... (or next successive weak link, depending on state of health...)

I'd suggest going to [http://www.antennasearch.com/sitestart.asp](http://www.antennasearch.com/sitestart.asp) and plugging in your home address. This is a national database that will tell you how many various sized cell towers, broadcast antennas, etc. are within a 4 mile radius of any address in the USA. (Consider adding these figures together to provide you a rough 'power level' for your environment's microwave that is beyond your immediate control)

After you've looked at your results, please try plugging in: Little Dry Rd, Cohagen, MT 59322. This search doesn't pull a direct result, but on the next page select the third option down: **Cohagen, Montana, United States**, then click process.

The more non-native EMF around you, the harder your system has to work to prevent breakdown/disease. I don't know your address, but I am 100% certain that there are more cell towers and antenna in your antennasearch result than in the Cohagen, MT result - since there are zero there when I checked .

So consider 'your' personal situation - let's say you are seemingly doing 'everything right' - CTing, RO water, HFLC seafood-heavy diet, magnetico, mitigating blue light at night, sleeping well, following the sun or a firm circadian rhythm, etc.

If your microwave's 'power level' is running at 500, but all of the activities you are doing only prepare your cell's to handle a maximum 'power level' of 400, then you are still leaving your fleshpod in a deficit exposure to a 'power level' of -100 delivering chaos and wreaking havok on your cells.

Each fleshpod is VERY unique - as in 100% unique from every other fleshpod that has ever existed - no two matter bodies can exist in the same place at the same time in this reality, so it is literally impossible to perfectly duplicate a lifetime's environmental exposure. This means that if two people are doing the same 'everything right', one of them may be able to handle a much stronger 'power level' in their environmental microwave than the other, just due to the fact that their fleshpod responds better to what they're doing. This is why you may look around and see people living healthly lives in the same environment as others who cannot maintain health.

So what does that mean? If you are doing everything you are able (...willing) to, but still are not able to halt progression and/or reverse disease, then the only viable conclusion is, your fleshpod isn't capable of handling your environment's 'power level' effectively even given the fact you are doing 'everything right'. I see three clear potential options, though there are truly infinite:
1 - do more to mitigate the full effect of your current environment's 'power level' of non-native EMF
2 - put your fleshpod in an environment that what you're currently doing is sufficient to fully mitigate AND begin reversing disease
3 - continue status quo, expecting status quo results

Consider that by and large this is a site for inquiring into the nature of "reality". It is not a "get free medical treatment and advice site". It is illegal to give medical advice in this context. We are struggling with the identification of the best conceptual framework to assess our current COE's and make relevant choices to optimize ourselves here. Our individual "personalities" and "communication styles" are secondary and in fact irrelevant. It is important to understand when and if one needs an actual "healthcare practitioner/guide" to work through one's own relevant and pressing issues. Even a brilliant "understanding of the game" will not make one a great "player". A physicist may understand football better than a quaterback, but he would not survive 10 seconds in a game.

### Brother John — 2014-11-20T21:48:00+0200

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=147960)
>
> Do you stop when you're tired or when you're done? Most people give up just before they break through to the next level. At the end of the day it's consistency that wins. Life is not a PG feel-good movie. Real life often ends badly for some. It's not always going to be rainbows and candy canes. Tough times don't last, but prepared people do! It's enduring through the tough times that builds character. No one wants to go through it, but we are better off once we do. It's just a 'season' that we go through. As we know seasons change. Make sure you are planting positive seeds in 'your springtime' so you can grow your relationships throughout the year.

Jack,
In the blog you said:"This also points out why those advocating a low carb high fat diet who have bad mitochondria might be dispensing bad public information. There is a deep down side to these dietary templates and that is the lesson I am trying to teach you here today. Context is critical when you understand how a mitochondria works."
But are not you advocating this same type of diet with increased DHA? The only person that I remember you saying could not do the low car high fat diet was Jeremy. He was the exception! Otherwise it was "Primordial" and would be a better way for just about anyone. Now it appears that you have widened that opinion to include those with trashed mitochondria. This may be true but you kind of just dropped that "Boom" in with everything else. I think this really interested Pat and it is very interesting to me also.
My experiments with myself indicate that I Don't know how much carbs I need or and how much carbs I can "get away with" before my system goes down hill. I read and experience advantages of low carb but also some difficulties. So my not final conclusion is this: Carb req may vary...
For you it seems that Wine and spices are "given a pass" in the "eat with the seasons" philosophy.
Any expansion on this topic, i.e. trashed mitochondria and low car high fat diet difficulties appreciated.
Also Thanks for some recent very intense, dense, informative, thought provoking and confusing for me, Blogs!!
Thanks,
Brother John

Yay, 6am in Cairns, and I wake up to another wave of information . Now I need to jump into the sea (literally and figuratively, another benefit of having moved out of crowded and busy Melbourne to a beach-side town where nobody gives a f\*\*\*\*)

One of my personal mottos:

Our job is to explain what we see.
Not to see only what we can explain.​

@[Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/) is doing a shitty job being a "cult leader" by pointing out observations without offering "canonical" explanations like a good preacher would.

\----

Remember that reductionism claims that: a System is the **Sum** of it's parts

This has nothing to do with the mechanistic behaviour of each part, which is usually deterministic.

For example: you can say for sure that the next electron to fill a ground-state Sulfur atom will enter the 4p orbital.

Determinism extends to a higher order -- meta-determinism.

Say for example: I can say very surely that a hydrogen atom can tunnel away from it's current carbon atom under a set of conditions A, B, C, D, and E. If only conditions A, B, C, and D are met, the tunnelling probabilities change. Now we're taking about a state machine that can move to one of many "pre-determined states", but does so based on a probabilistic model of information influx which we can reproduce experimentally.

Therefore, in experimentally repeatable mechanistic behaviour of the components of the system I trust.

To discern the emergent (and exponentially **multiplicative**) behaviour of those parts I must experiment (on myself).

Now onto diving into some of the parts before my daily swim .......

.

The difference between a cult and an ideological movement is epistemological. A "cult" is a group organized around absolute loyalty to a person as opposed to set of ideas. Even a radical set of ideas does not define a cult, whether the ideas are true or false. An organization built around blind loyality to a leader in deference to any facts is what defines a cult. Epistemology is a necessary tool to discern the difference. Have you read my [blog](http://jackkruse.com/tensegrity-3-epistemology-vs-social-dogma/)???

**FAD Moiety
**
In the blog, it was mentioned that:

When blue light hits the FAD moiety it knocks out an electron out of the FAD part of cytochrome 1 which generates a quantum signal in the FeS cluster N1a.

This is sent to other tissues and the brain to precisely yoke the seasonal photons in the blue range with to keep the synchrony with the SCN in the brain and body clocks in tissues and the liver. When this signaling is broken all hell breaks loose.
​

Now this is one place where I currently do not see the full picture.

The FAD and FADH2 redox reaction is reversible, and involves the exchange of 2 protons and 2 electrons. The proton side of the equation of FAD is not mentioned directly in the blog.

(remember that FADH2 is one of the important electron inputs for Oxidative Phosphorylation -- [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oxidative\_phosphorylation](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oxidative_phosphorylation))

Right now I'm assuming that blue light hits FAD, and provides the electron side of the input. Now you have a proton sink that needs to be filled from some source. Another reason to pump protons out of mitochondria?

Another note is that FAD is an aromatic ring, while the oxidised FADH2 is much more polar (and easier to transport electromagnetically back into the magnets we call mitochondria?)

### yewwei.tan — 2014-11-21T01:25:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-9-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12199/post-148033>

> [yewwei.tan said:](/goto/post?id=148019)
>
> **F-Actin Networks Reading**

From -- [http://www.sciencemag.org/content/304/5675/1301.full](http://www.sciencemag.org/content/304/5675/1301.full)

The mechanical properties, or elasticity, of these cytoskeletal networks are intimately involved in determining how forces are generated and transmitted in living cells. In vitro, actin can polymerize to form long rigid filaments (F-actin), with a diameter _D_ ≈ 7 nm and contour lengths up to _L_ ≈ 20 μm

Entangled solutions of F-actin are a well-studied model for semiflexible polymers; they exhibit exceptionally large elasticity at small volume fractions as compared with more traditional flexible polymers at similar concentrations ([_4_](http://www.sciencemag.org/content/304/5675/1301.full#ref-4)–[_6_](http://www.sciencemag.org/content/304/5675/1301.full#ref-6)).

However, in vivo, the cytoskeletal network is regulated and controlled not only by the concentration of F-actin, but also by accessory proteins that bind to F-actin. Indeed, nature provides a host of actin-binding proteins (ABPs) with a wide range of functions:

We study the elasticity of a model composite network of bundled and cross-linked F-actin. We use an ABP that simultaneously bundles and cross-links actin filaments while being sufficiently rigid so as not to contribute to the network compliance.

For example, by changing the cross-link concentration alone, the network elastic modulus can be reliably and precisely tuned over more than three decades ([Fig. 1A](http://www.sciencemag.org/content/304/5675/1301.full#F1));

(_TYW: "decades" mean a power of 10 variation in magnitude. So 3 decades means a 1000-fold change_)

Most ABPs implicated in cross-linking or bundling the network are themselves highly dynamic and compliant, making it extremely difficult to isolate the origins of elasticity of the F-actin network itself

To overcome these difficulties, and define a benchmark for the elasticity of cross-linked actin networks, we use scruin, which is found in the sperm cell of the horseshoe crab

In vivo, scruin mediates the formation of a single, crystalline bundle of 80 actin filaments; this functions as a mechanochemical spring in the acrosomal process

In the absence of scruin, an F-actin solution forms an isotropic and homogeneous mesh; at a concentration of 11.9 μM, the mean separation between filaments is ∼300 nm

We tune the degree of filament cross-linking and bundling by varying the scruin concentration, _c_S, for a fixed actin concentration, _c_A, such that the density of cross-links, _R_, is _c_S/_c_A and varies from 0 to 1

The morphology of actin-scruin networks remains similar to that of an entangled F-actin solution with values of _R_ up to 0.03. For _R_ > 0.03, we observe the formation of compact bundles ([Fig. 1C](http://www.sciencemag.org/content/304/5675/1301.full#F1))

the bundle thickness increases even further as _R_ is increased, as shown for _R_ = 0.5 in [Fig. 1D](http://www.sciencemag.org/content/304/5675/1301.full#F1)

To measure the elasticity of these networks, we apply a force and measure the resultant deformation; the elastic modulus, _G_′, is defined as the ratio of the stress, σ, or force per unit area, to the strain, γ; thus, _G_′ = σ/γ

The networks can also have a viscous or dissipative response, and the resultant stress depends on the strain rate, { "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

, defining the loss modulus,

. More generally, _G_′(ω) and _G_″(ω) depend on the frequency and are measured by applying a small oscillatory stress at a frequency, ω. In these cross-linked networks, the elastic modulus dominates the mechanical response, reaching a frequency-independent plateau, _G_o, at frequencies less than 1 Hz (fig. S1).

The elasticity of the networks can also exhibit a marked dependence on applied strain, γ. In the regime where _G_o increases rapidly with _R_ ([Fig. 1A](http://www.sciencemag.org/content/304/5675/1301.full#F1)), the elastic modulus is linear below a critical strain, γcrit ∼ 10%. However, above this strain, _G_o increases dramatically over a very small change in γ up to a maximum strain, γmax, whereupon the network breaks irreversibly ([Fig. 2B](http://www.sciencemag.org/content/304/5675/1301.full#F2), inset)

This strain stiffening is completely reversible and the network can be cycled through strains up to γmax without any history dependence of the elastic response. These composite networks constitute an exceptional material, whose modulus increases significantly with virtually no change in γ; thus, they have a nearly vanishing compliance for γ>γcrit.

The elasticity arises because an applied strain reduces the accessible fluctuations; this is an entropic elasticity, similar to that of flexible polymers such as rubber.

However, when filaments are chemically cross-linked into networks such that the distance between cross-links,

c, is less than

p, or when the persistence length is increased as a result of bundling, it is unclear whether the network elasticity originates from the actual bending of filaments, which is an enthalpic elasticity, or from stretching out thermal fluctuations of the filaments, which is an entropic elasticity

To delineate the role of entropic effects in network elasticity, we model the network as a collection of thermally fluctuating, semiflexible filament segments of length,

, the average distance between cross-links, such that

.

Thermally driven transverse fluctuations reduce the end-to-end filament extension and lead to an entropic spring; for small extensions, the force, _F_, required to extend this spring by length

is

, where _k_B_T_ is the thermal energy ([_12_](http://www.sciencemag.org/content/304/5675/1301.full#ref-12)) (fig. S2)

We conclude that the elastic stiffness of both weakly cross-linked and thickly bundled actin networks is entropic in origin, originating from the stretching out of thermal fluctuations of individual actin filaments ([Fig. 3](http://www.sciencemag.org/content/304/5675/1301.full#F3)).

​

### yewwei.tan — 2014-11-21T01:25:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-9-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12199/post-148034>

(continued)

A critical test of this picture for the origin of the elasticity is the strain dependence; when an entropic, semiflexible network is extended, thermally induced transverse fluctuations are pulled ou

When the elastic modulus is strain-independent, the differential modulus is the same as the elastic modulus, _K_′(σo) = _G_o. However, above the critical stress, σcrit = _G_oγcrit, _K_′ increases markedly until the network breaks, as shown for a large range of actin concentrations at fixed _R_ in [Fig. 4](http://www.sciencemag.org/content/304/5675/1301.full#F4).

This provides conclusive support for our hypothesis that the origin of both the linear and the nonlinear elasticity of the network is due to stretching of thermal fluctuations of single filaments. Thus, the elastic stiffening of the network results from the nonlinear force-extension behavior of individual filaments; this further supports our hypothesis that the elasticity of cross-linked actin networks is entropic in origin

Because network elasticity is determined by filament bending, which is rather small on the molecular scale, there is no mechanism for strain stiffening. Thus, as summarized in [Fig. 5](http://www.sciencemag.org/content/304/5675/1301.full#F5), a network of rigidly cross-linked semiflexible polymers can exhibit a rich variety of elastic behaviors simply by varying the filament concentration, cross-link density, or bundle thickness.

Our results also have important implications for how a cell may regulate its mechanical response, with small variations in either filament or cross-link concentration to make rapid and precise transitions to qualitatively alter the mechanical strength of the cytoskeletal network.

​

This concept of "Entropic Elasticity" is very important. Even the wikipedia article describing this concept illustrates some key concepts -- [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rubber\_elasticity](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rubber_elasticity)

Elastic polymers do **not** store any potential energy in stretched chemical bonds or elastic work done in stretching molecules, when work is done upon them. Instead, all work done on the rubber is "released" (not stored) and appears immediately in the polymer as thermal energy

The ability of an elastomer to do work depends (as with an ideal gas) only on entropy-change considerations, and not on any stored (i.e., potential) energy within the polymer bonds. Instead, the energy to do work comes entirely from thermal energy, and (as in the case of an expanding ideal gas) only the positive entropy change of the polymer allows its internal thermal energy to be converted efficiently (100% in theory) into work

Temperature affects the elasticity of elastomers in an unusual way. When the elastomer is assumed to be in a stretched state, heating causes them to contract. Vice versa, cooling can cause expansion.​

Sounds exactly like water doesn't it? And the sources of thermal energy are obviously very abundant (mitochondria themselves, DHA transduction, etc ....)

Now, the more cross-linked actin is, the higher percentage of the water that is trapped in the network is charge separated water. The fact that this network dissipates any incoming infrared energy so well makes the likelihood of EZ water formation even greater.

There is still a need for the actin filaments to remain hydrophilic of course, and this requires electrons. How do you get electrons into the network? DHA? Positively charged "holes" by generation of large proton gradients? Distances are measured in the 100s of nanometres too, which is not suitable for direct tunnelling effects ....

Don't know yet, more reading required .....

.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-11-21T02:19:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-9-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12199/post-148035>

Now, here's another paper '**Interheme electron tunneling in cytochrome c oxidase**' -- [http://www.pnas.org/content/107/50/21470.long](http://www.pnas.org/content/107/50/21470.long)

Here we show by independent molecular dynamics and quantum-chemical methods that the heme-heme eT in C_c_O differs from the majority of cases in having an **exceptionally low reorganization energy**​

The electrons and protons are transferred from opposite sides of the membrane; the former from the soluble electron carrier cytochrome _c_ at the positively charged P-side of the membrane, and the latter from the negatively charged N-side of the membrane ([Fig. 1](http://www.pnas.org/content/107/50/21470.long#F1)).

This charge movement establishes an electrochemical proton gradient across the membrane.

In addition, C_c_O couples the electron transfer to uptake of a second proton per transferred electron, from the N-side of the membrane, which is pumped across to the P-side, thus doubling the efficiency of the classical Mitchellian redox-loop mechanism

In short, by reducing molecular oxygen to water, C_c_O couples the released energy to pump protons across the mitochondrial membrane.

(_TYW: heme a link -- [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heme\_A](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heme_A)_)

The behavior of the electron is bound to follow the laws of the quantum world, whereas the proton generally behaves in a more classical manner. Due to this difference, electrons have the ability to tunnel through long distances of space, whereas proton tunneling is spatially significantly more restricted.

For example, the di-copper center CuA in C_c_O is located at a center-to-center distance of ∼20 _Å_ from heme _a_ that accepts the electrons from CuA

(_TYW: this distance is dangerously close to the 14 Angstrom limit to electron tunnelling_)

Proton transfer often takes place by the so-called Grotthuss mechanism, where the charge rather than the proton itself is transferred through a hydrogen-bonded network

Electron transfer in C_c_O takes place from the primary electron acceptor site CuA to the low-spin heme _a_, and further to the binuclear heme _a_3/CuB site of oxygen reduction

Electron transfer from heme _a_ to the binuclear site is linked to a **coupled proton movement from Glu-242**[\*](http://www.pnas.org/content/107/50/21470.long#fn-6), located at the end of the proton conducting D-channel, to a proton-loading site (PLS) located “above” heme _a_3 ([2](http://www.pnas.org/content/107/50/21470.long#ref-2), [6](http://www.pnas.org/content/107/50/21470.long#ref-6), [7](http://www.pnas.org/content/107/50/21470.long#ref-7)).

Mainly due to the coupled proton movements, the interheme electron transfer rate shows wide variations, ranging from nano- to milliseconds

The rate of nonadiabatic electron transfer can be described by the semiclassical Marcus theory ([16](http://www.pnas.org/content/107/50/21470.long#ref-16)–[19](http://www.pnas.org/content/107/50/21470.long#ref-19)) as,

where Δ_G_ is the driving force of the reaction, _HAB_ is the electronic coupling between donor and acceptor, λ is the reorganization energy,

The **reorganization energy describes the energy required to relax the environment upon electron transfer,** i.e., the energy difference between the oxidized system at the reduced and oxidized structures, and vice versa for the reduced system.

Moser et al. ([20](http://www.pnas.org/content/107/50/21470.long#ref-20)) derived an empirical relation between the rate of exergonic biological electron transfer and the distance between electron donor and acceptor, which has proved to be very successful.

where _R_ is the edge-to-edge donor-acceptor distance (D-A) (in Å) and the energies are expressed in eV.

The factor 0.6 reflects the experimentally observed mean effect of the protein medium between donor and acceptor and the factor 3.1 includes a quantum-mechanical adjustment of the Marcus treatment ([21](http://www.pnas.org/content/107/50/21470.long#ref-21), [22](http://www.pnas.org/content/107/50/21470.long#ref-22)). As (Δ_G_ + _λ_)2/4_λ_ is the activation energy for the eT reaction according to Marcus theory, activationless eT is obtained at _λ_ = -Δ_G_.

Subsequently, a second version of this equation was derived ([23](http://www.pnas.org/content/107/50/21470.long#ref-23)) to include possible variations in the intervening protein medium,

Here, _ρ_ is the effective protein packing density that may vary between 0 (vacuum) and 1 (full packing). Based on extensive studies of different electron transfer proteins it was argued that the **value for _ρ_ is relatively invariant (_ca._ 0.76)**, and especially that **packing does not increase to compensate for the decrease in eT rate at long distances.**

On the basis of experiments where the effect of driving force (Δ_G_) on the eT rate was studied in photosynthetic systems, it was further argued that for most cases the above formula could be further simplified by using a generic value for λ of about 0.7 eV ([23](http://www.pnas.org/content/107/50/21470.long#ref-23)), with a notable exception of the initial charge separation in photosynthetic reaction centers, where λ is ∼0.3 eV ([24](http://www.pnas.org/content/107/50/21470.long#ref-24)).

Employing the Moser-Dutton formula in [Eq. **2**](http://www.pnas.org/content/107/50/21470.long#disp-formula-2) for interheme electron tunneling in C_c_O, using the generic values for reorganization energy and packing density discussed above, and an edge-to-edge distance of 7 Å, yields a rate of 1/1.4 ns-1, which is in remarkable agreement with experiment

However, recent assessments of the reorganization energy based on measurements of the eT reaction rate and driving force at different temperatures, and on a quantum-mechanically modified expression of [Eq. **1**](http://www.pnas.org/content/107/50/21470.long#disp-formula-1), suggested that λ may be as low as 0.2 eV ([14](http://www.pnas.org/content/107/50/21470.long#ref-14)), which would predict a rate of 1/40 ps-1 according to the Moser-Dutton formula.

To explain this discrepancy, it was suggested that the packing density might be lower than the used generic value ([15](http://www.pnas.org/content/107/50/21470.long#ref-15)), or that eT follows specifically evolved tunneling pathways

On the other hand, as pointed out by Moser et al. ([27](http://www.pnas.org/content/107/50/21470.long#ref-27)), the temperature range covered in the experimental study ([14](http://www.pnas.org/content/107/50/21470.long#ref-14)) may not have been sufficient to exclude a much higher value of λ.

\---

As a further exploration, we present the results of two independent methods of calculating the reorganization energy for the heme _a_/heme _a_3 eT from molecular simulations

By using the formalism developed by Warshel et al. ([28](http://www.pnas.org/content/107/50/21470.long#ref-28)–[35](http://www.pnas.org/content/107/50/21470.long#ref-35)), and later verified from first-principle statistical mechanics ([36](http://www.pnas.org/content/107/50/21470.long#ref-36), [37](http://www.pnas.org/content/107/50/21470.long#ref-37)), we have used classical molecular dynamics (MD) simulations and quantum-mechanically derived point charge distributions to estimate the reorganization energy by the energy-gap approach (see [_Methods_](http://www.pnas.org/content/107/50/21470.long#sec-2)).

In a second independent approach, we calculate the reorganization energy by using high-level quantum-mechanical (QM) density functional theory on realistically sized models of the heme groups.
​

**By both methods we find that the reorganization energy is, indeed, very low.**

We find that the average ρ is close to the proposed generic value, but that the donor-acceptor distance distribution is relatively broad. By combining the determined reorganization energy with our alternative treatment of ρ and _R_ in [Eq. **3**](http://www.pnas.org/content/107/50/21470.long#disp-formula-3), we obtain an eT rate in excellent agreement with the experimentally measured values.
​

(continued in next post)

​

### yewwei.tan — 2014-11-21T02:19:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-9-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12199/post-148036>

**Results and Discussion**

The obtained reorganization energy for the process is 0.20 eV for both the forward (heme _a_ → heme _a_3) and backward (heme _a_3 → heme _a_) reactions.

The reorganization energy obtained from high-level quantum-chemical calculations is 0.13 eV using the model systems shown in [Fig. 3](http://www.pnas.org/content/107/50/21470.long#F3), which agrees surprisingly well with the value obtained from the MD simulations.

The electrostatic effect of the surroundings omitted from the QM system, modeled as point-charges, was found to have a minute effect of decreasing the computed reorganization energy by ∼0.003 eV.

(_TYW: so increased gravity, which is basically the electrostatic force, allows for electron tunnelling mechanisms to relax faster and therefore tunnel electrons at a faster rate_)

When the surrounding protein environment is frozen to an equilibrated structure of the fully oxidized system in the MD simulations, the reorganization energy increases to 0.33 and 0.32 eV for the forward and backward eT, respectively ([Fig. 2](http://www.pnas.org/content/107/50/21470.long#F2)_B_). When, in addition, the interactions to the water molecules are switched off, the reorganization energy increases to 0.52 and 0.50 eV, respectively ([Fig. 2](http://www.pnas.org/content/107/50/21470.long#F2)_C_), **emphasizing the important function of the quite large amount of water in the immediate surrounding of the hemes.**

The reorganization energies seem to be well converged within 1 ns simulations time as indicated in [Fig. 2](http://www.pnas.org/content/107/50/21470.long#F2)_D_.

\---

It is of interest to estimate the possible sources for errors in the calculations.

Employing a nonpolarizable force field has recently been shown to lead to too high reorganization energies ([38](http://www.pnas.org/content/107/50/21470.long#ref-38)).

In a previous computational study, Sigfridsson et al. ([39](http://www.pnas.org/content/107/50/21470.long#ref-39)), while noting similar reorganization energies for low-spin heme models, found significantly higher values for high-spin heme models compared to our QM results.

A possible reason is that the protein environment imposes a strain on the redox group, leading to smaller structural changes relative to a vacuum optimization, a possibility supported by optimization of the metal centers embedded in their native protein environments

In particular, **polarization of water molecules during eT is of profound importance (see [Fig. S1](http://www.pnas.org/lookup/suppl/doi:10.1073/pnas.1005889107/-/DCSupplemental/pnas.1005889107_SI.pdf?targetid=SF1)).**

The probability of the water dipole vector pointing towards the iron atom of heme _a_ is high at close distance in the fully oxidized state, and it drops nearly as 1/_r_ towards 0.3, which is obtained at 13 Å.

When heme _a_ is reduced, the distribution of water orientations changes dramatically; the probability of finding a dipole orientated towards heme _a_ is nearly zero at distances less than 11 Å from the iron atom of heme _a_.

When the electron resides on heme _a_3 this probability increases rapidly. In contrast to the strong orientational dependence of water dipoles on the redox state of heme _a_, water molecules do not seem to prefer such a strong redox-state dependent orientation towards heme _a_3, which suggest that this site is nearly in a prereorganized environment.

Our results can be compared with the analytical expression for the reorganization energy derived from continuum theory ([16](http://www.pnas.org/content/107/50/21470.long#ref-16)),

where _rD_ and _rA_ are the radii of the electron donor and acceptor, _R_ is the distance between the donor and the acceptor groups, and _ε_op and ϵ are the optical and static dielectric constants of the medium

For heme _a_ and heme _a_3, the radii can be taken as 6 Å, which are approximately the distance from the iron to the edge of the porphyrin ring. The hemes are separated by 7 Å, and the dielectric constant between the hemes has recently been estimated to be _ca._ 4

This crude approximation gives a reorganization energy of 0.1 eV using an optical dielectric constant of 2

The low reorganization energy predicted by the continuum theoretical treatment is based on the proximity of the electron donor and acceptor. When the groups are sufficiently close in space, they share a common polarization of the environment.

(_TYW: what affects the distance between Cyt c1 and CcO?_)

The average packing density ρ, obtained by summing over all possible atomic donor-acceptor pairs, is 0.76, which is identical to the generic value used by Page et al.

\---

By employing [Eq. **3**](http://www.pnas.org/content/107/50/21470.long#disp-formula-3), but computing an individual eT rate for each donor-acceptor pair, with individual packing densities for the intervening media and _λ_ = 0.2 eV, we obtain an average tunneling rate of 1/1.8 ± 1/0.3 ns-1 (averaged over the rate, _k_) in excellent agreement with the experimental data

For comparison, the generic reorganization energy of 0.7 eV would give a rate of 1/60 ± 1/11 ns-1 by this methodology.

\---

A key reaction in the proton-pumping process is the reduction of heme _a_, which increases the pK_a_ of the PLS (proton loading site) ([2](http://www.pnas.org/content/107/50/21470.long#ref-2), [43](http://www.pnas.org/content/107/50/21470.long#ref-43)). The PLS should hence be located as close to heme _a_ as possible.

On the other hand, the PLS must also be close to the binuclear site (heme _a_3) because releasing the proton from the PLS is electrostatically driven by uptake of the ”substrate proton” into that site ([2](http://www.pnas.org/content/107/50/21470.long#ref-2)).

In C_c_O the fast intrinsic rate of electron tunneling between the hemes may be of special functional significance for two reasons. First, the rapid tunneling rate may help trapping the bound O2 molecule to the binuclear site by eT

In addition, fast eT between heme _a_ and the binuclear site will effectively trap the proton to be pumped to the PLS, thus minimizing its back-leakage ([43](http://www.pnas.org/content/107/50/21470.long#ref-43)), because after the eT the barrier for proton back-transfer is raised considerably ([2](http://www.pnas.org/content/107/50/21470.long#ref-2), [8](http://www.pnas.org/content/107/50/21470.long#ref-8), [43](http://www.pnas.org/content/107/50/21470.long#ref-43)).

As the activation energy of eT increases, it might become a competing process with proton back-leakage from the PLS ([48](http://www.pnas.org/content/107/50/21470.long#ref-48)).

On the other hand, a slower proton-loading step might lead to a decrease in _k_cat. In summary, maximizing the rate of eT may help to minimize energy losses in an energy-transducing machine such as C_c_O ([49](http://www.pnas.org/content/107/50/21470.long#ref-49)).

\---

We conclude that both a MD and a quantum chemical approach finds a reorganization energy for electron tunneling between hemes _a_ and _a_3 of _ca._ 0.2 eV, in agreement with the earlier experimental study

​

These results obviously have huge implications for Complex III function -- [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Coenzyme\_Q\_–\_cytochrome\_c\_reductase](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Coenzyme_Q_%E2%80%93_cytochrome_c_reductase)

The diagrams there also demonstrate how leakiness between Complex III and IV can lead to radical formation, and the loss of water.

You need a proton gradient for CytC to work. Why the hell? It goes on to make an even bigger proton gradient ....

Is the purpose of concentrating iron when redox is poor in effect decreasing the distances between electron donor and acceptor? (Cyt C1 and CcO)

I will need to let these diagrams sit in my head for awhile before commenting further.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-11-21T02:48:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-9-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12199/post-148040>

Linking back to this post about chocolate -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/chocolate-and-gut-microbiome.10256/page-2#post-147152](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/chocolate-and-gut-microbiome.10256/page-2#post-147152)

At its core, the mammalian Hippo pathway is composed of two kinases, namely MST and LATS. They control the activity of two closely related transcriptional co-activators YAP and TAZ.

How do this tensegrity system work? When MST and LATS kinases respond to various upstream stimuli, the MST-activated LATS kinase phosphorylates YAP and TAZ at a specific Serine residue located in the amino-terminal region of each protein.

Following this, 14-3-3 proteins recognize a signature motif around the LATS-phosphorylated Serine, which allows strong protein–protein interactions to be established. The resulting complex then “anchors” or sequesters YAP and TAZ within the cytoplasm.

What changes the signaling in the AA protein polymers I just mentioned? electrons and protons in superconducting cables that surround actin filaments and integrins components.
​

Hippo Pathway -- [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hippo\_signaling\_pathway](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hippo_signaling_pathway)

Paper about YAP, LATS, and F-Action -- [http://www.molbiolcell.org/content/25/10/1676.long](http://www.molbiolcell.org/content/25/10/1676.long)

From this study, as well as from several recent reports, it is clear that LATS phosphorylation of AMOT130 inhibits its ability to bind F-actin ([Adler _et al._, 2013b](http://www.molbiolcell.org/content/25/10/1676.long#ref-2); [Chan _et al._, 2013](http://www.molbiolcell.org/content/25/10/1676.long#ref-5); [Dai _et al._, 2013](http://www.molbiolcell.org/content/25/10/1676.long#ref-6); [Hirate _et al._, 2013](http://www.molbiolcell.org/content/25/10/1676.long#ref-13)). We show that LATS phosphorylation blocks AMOT130 binding to F-actin, allowing it to bind YAP and sequester it in the cytoplasm.
​

So LATS phosphorylation basically destroys the F-Actin filament network and causes cell swelling .... which has the direct result of making the F-Actin and Integrin network very inefficient that transducing signals.

Now the question is what happens next? What does the body do to try and amplify the signal when the transmission medium starts to breakdown?

My intuitive thought is to start using heavy polar particles that can be transported in plasma and attracted to areas where magnetic flux is failing. It's less efficient that electron and proton wave entanglement, but it can get the job done.
