### NeilBB — 2014-11-24T05:07:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-9-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12199/post-148313>

> [[email redacted] said:](/goto/post?id=148312)
>
> Um...............ah...............I 'try' to (know my limitations) sometimes I think I am TOO much that way. I tend to see the 'holes' in my own thinking and I suppose by extension then to others too. I am skeptical to a fault. See when I see you say so confidently that you know your limitations in my mind that kind of means you actually don't. If you REALLY did you would not be so sure. But neither one is 'right' being crippled by doubt is no fun either and I would not necessarily recommend it and good for you for being confident in yourself. But I don't think it has that much to do with 'epistemology' it is more of your or my instinctive feeling about life and ourselves

Okay, so you are a philosophical skeptic, like Hume. We can't know if the sun will ever rise, right? That is your bias. I reject that kind of skepticism. I don't regard one's having contextual certainty as necessarily diagnostic of narcissism. On your terms, no one can ever be certain of anything. Your standard of epistemology is "doubt." Doubt doesn't build bridges. That view is self-refuting. Even David Hume didn't live by his skepticism, people said he was a nice regular guy...

Inductive knowledge is possible. It is limited and contextual and subject to certain types of revision, but it is still possible. You have to start at the observational level and build abstractions by a specific process. That is my point.

Skepticism is good to employ to a point. But you also have to know when it's time to draw a conclusion. I would maintain that when to be skeptical and when to be assertive are epistemological issues and that epistemology is the science of human knowledge and is actually neither arbitrary nor subject to random opinions. I can't possibly prove that to you in this thread, but it is the basis of my mental self-confidence that you now question...

### yewwei.tan — 2014-11-24T05:28:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-9-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12199/post-148314>

> [[email redacted] said:](/goto/post?id=148312)
>
> Um...............ah...............I 'try' to (know my limitations) sometimes I think I am TOO much that way. I tend to see the 'holes' in my own thinking and I suppose by extension then to others too. I am skeptical to a fault. See when I see you say so confidently that you know your limitations in my mind that kind of means you actually don't. If you REALLY did you would not be so sure. But neither one is 'right' being crippled by doubt is no fun either and I would not necessarily recommend it and good for you for being confident in yourself. But I don't think it has that much to do with 'epistemology' it is more of your or my instinctive feeling about life and ourselves

Do you want to win? Or do you just want to win arguments?

I personally just want to discover, understand, and implement the things that will lead to best outcomes for myself and for the people I try to help.

Skepticism is harmful if it prevents exploration. Believing something is harmless if it doesn't cost you much; believe anything you can afford to believe.

The one and only lesson that I learnt in University was from my Math Lecturer, who began every single statement of theory with the words, "If you believe X, .... then Y must be true".

The thinker who is obsessed with trying to find the correct answer and applying that to real life scenarios should first learn how to postulate new beliefs, be aware of those beliefs, and then ask "what if X were true"?

For me, I like skeptics for the same reason Jack does -- they will actively shoot down your new postulations, and help you figure out which ones are worth keeping.

Ending this rant with a quote from Nassim Nicholas Taleb:

_Sadly, we learn the most from fools, economists, and other reverse role models, yet we pay them back with the worst ingratitude_
​

... which pretty much sums up my attitude towards so called skeptics

### yewwei.tan — 2014-11-24T06:18:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-9-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12199/post-148315>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=148253)
>
> So how did he figure it out using ideas in his head that were directionally accurate? He used the quantum computer in his head........to create more possibilites than the math could provide. He then realize _something simple_ about Helium.
>
> Superfluidity only occurs in liquid [helium-4](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Helium-4) at far higher temperatures than it does in [helium-3](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Helium-3). This was when I realized I had to understand temperature's effects on atoms carefully. What one atom does in one environments is not what it can do in others. Feynman gave me the insight to bio hack the periodic table for life. I looked at every atom in biochemistry and began to look at it like no one else has. In helium's case Feynman realized that each atom of helium-4 is a [boson](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Boson) particle, by virtue of its zero spin. Here is when I realized spin was a big assed deal. I then learned on my own that Helium-3, a different isotope of helium, is a [fermion](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fermion) particle, This meant something that science says is the same from a label really was not in the real world. And when something can be a chameleon life uses these elements to their nth degree to create the spark of life. Helium is not part of this because of one reason only. Helium is not found on Earth in enough quantity. But today our government is hoarding it. I find that most interesting. Helium 3 the fermion rogue element, can form bosons only by pairing **with itself** at much lower temperatures, in a process similar to the electron pairing in [superconductivity](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Superconductivity). I told you in the OSF series how evolution began......it was the sexual attraction of like like each other where fractals began and it still persists today. Although most dont see what I see yet...........kinetic isotope effects are interesting issues in the periodic table.

> [NeilBB said:](/goto/post?id=148276)
>
> I think Pollack's concept of "like likes like" can probably somehow be expanded to explain the so-called "strong nuclear force," in other words how the protons all stick together in atomic nuclei other than hydrogen. Clearly, the positioning of the neutrons would have to be key. I can see the neutrons taking on something like the role of "iodine" that you have previously described, in similarly organizing these structures of multiple protons... There would of-course be no EZ-water inside an atom, but there certainly could be stored energy and perhaps a form of "sub-protonic charge-separation" involving the quarks... Then, you might be able to potentially explain quantum chromodynamics in terms of quantum electrodynamics... It's just that nature's fundamental postive charges are 1/3 of the size of nature's fundamental negative charges. There likely needed to be such an asymmetry of charge so that matter could organize at all... (electrical symmetry-breaking at the subatomic level)

Pollack talks a little about Feynman's like-likes-like idea, and about how you need an "unlike" charge carrier between the "like" charge carriers -- [http://faculty.washington.edu/ghp/research-themes/origin-of-life/](http://faculty.washington.edu/ghp/research-themes/origin-of-life/)

I like the idea Neil (quoted above) about this possibly explaining the strong force.

Should also note that the Carbon atom nucleus is considered a boson, and that bosons with the same energy try to occupy the same space.

Cooling has the effect of condensing bosons in the sense that if you reduce the number of possible energetic states of the bosons, then they will statistically be more bosons with the same energy, and naturally these bosons will try to occupy the same space. This condensation does actually result in a small amount of actual volume condensation, but it mostly refers to a reduction in the number of energetic quanta that a boson can occupy.

How does this affect the distribution of charges? Boson crystal latticess? An even number fermions (half-spin) can form a boson (integer spin), so what of the transition metals which seem to mediate all these quantum mechanical effects -- Josephson junctions, SQUIDs, Cooper pairs, etc ....? I have no clue yet

Sidenote: I had recently re-read the Quantum Cell blog post, and it seems very relevant to these topics -- [http://jackkruse.com/ee-6-quantum-cell-theory-life-collective-phenomena/](http://jackkruse.com/ee-6-quantum-cell-theory-life-collective-phenomena/)

If so, I say congratulations! That is what I would call substantial progress. Dump the dogma and clear your mind for the challenge of new discovery, even if it makes you dizzy...

Fractals are one of those weird constructs which obviously exist in nature, but can be mathematically represented by non-real math (complex numbers).

I was wary of this fact right from the beginning, because the Math allows for fractals to live between dimensions (fractals have a fractional dimension), and Math also allows you to expand dimensions, allowing for some pretty far-fetched leaps into higher dimensions like time, linked by some self-similar fractal.

I like the word "self-similar" a lot more. One has to actually think about "what 2 or more things are self-similar?" before proceeding on to the next step.

Sidenote: although I can speak it and read it decently well, I hate the Chinese language in the way that it is used by most Chinese cultures. The language is very, very terse, which means that you can pack a lot of information into very few words. That makes the Chinese some of the worst hypocrites ever, being able to pay lip service to things that they have never done. (This generalisation requires a specific cultural context to hold true; the Chinese language itself isn't inherently harmful. The cultural context which I grew up in - Singapore - displays this abuse very evidently)​

Secondary Sidenote: I hope the word "fractal" doesn't inherit the same woo-woo notion that a word like "quantum" has in certain circles (thanks a lot Deepak Chopra  ....). "Quantum" just means a specific discrete point. Particles and Waves tend to be only able to exist with specific amounts of energy, not just any amount of energy they desire. They have specific "quanta" of energy, and which quantum (singular) state they manifest in depends on probabilistic factors.​

\----

Funny you bring the pyramids up. Those stones are indeed insanely big, and I think that they were moved by understanding magnetic and electric forces

I'm referring to friction, and I think it was Graham Hancock who has been recently talking about how the Egyptians had mastered ways of using ramps, sand, and water, in a lost field of engineering that could probably be called "sand fluid mechanics". Friction (and low friction) is just another electric phenomena (Gerald Pollack's 4th Phase of Water book talks about why ice is slippery).

This conjecture was made after some scientists found that you could massively reduce frictional . Another article I found giving this a brief mention -- [http://www.washingtonpost.com/news/...ive-pyramid-stones-without-modern-technology/](http://www.washingtonpost.com/news/morning-mix/wp/2014/05/02/the-surprisingly-simple-way-egyptians-moved-massive-pyramid-stones-without-modern-technology/)

Video illustrating the concept:

While we're on the topic of ancient civilisations, the stone carvings at Gobleki Tepe are incredibly complex, and basically impossible for the "stone tool wielding" wielding people of at least 12,000 years old (that's how old the site is)

More than one archeologist has commented that these were cut using extreme heat. We still wonder how these people managed to do it, though maybe some manipulation of the electromagnetic force is the most plausible idea (for some reason I had the silly idea of "giant magnifying glasses" used to focus solar radiation to a point and cause the heating needed to cut stone)

\-----

This point about stone moving is related to the question about the Sun.

First, if the above hypothesis about low-friction damp sand is true, we still need to ask where did all the water come from? One possibility is also that the region where these pyramids were created weren't as desert-ified as today, or maybe there weren't deserts at all.

Having Saturn as our sun would have created major differences in weather patterns for sure, on top of an entirely different celestial configuration as perceived by the humans on earth.

\----

Regarding stars, the Electric Universe Model would postulate that all stars all linked electrically by Birkeland Currents. It then doesn't make sense to say that the Sun is orbiting anything. Instead, we have to ask what are all the bodies which the sun has electric interaction with (either giving or receiving discharge), and how would those resulting forces lead to reconfiguration of the arrangement of the stars.

Or course, this is a dynamic system, and you also have the possibility of other huge bodies coming into electric interaction with our sun in the future, and changing the entire picture once again ....

\----

### NeilBB — 2014-11-25T01:56:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-9-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12199/post-148360>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=148355)
>
> I used light from Andromeda to discuss how powerful the electromagnetic force is. Today we see light from it that came before our species even evolved..........That is the definition of infinite power and range........

Infinite power. Infinite range. But not infinite speed.

Far from it, in fact, light waves move pretty slowly at the extreme scales of physics. "Gravitational waves" would have to be much faster than light to be able to hold the planets in orbit. And if there were some kind of "quantum waves" that explained entanglement and superposition, those waves would also have to seriously outrun light, wouldn't they?

So what breaks the "cosmic speed limit" and how does it do it? That is the real question you've been hinting at, isn't it?

QED, light, the photoelectric effect are about the motion of photons and electrons. All of that seems way way too slow for this.

But the other part of the EM force is magnetism. Magnetism is "relativistic" and mysterious and fundamental somehow to all matter. Superconducting magnets can openly defy gravity as you have emphasized.

So I'm going to postulate that the unification of the forces has more to do with magnetism than it does with light. Magnetism, like gravity, must be a fundamental emergent property of matter, and the two must be very closely interlinked?

If so, maybe magnetism and gravity are actually more fundamental than light, rather than the other way around? Makes sense because any subatomic particle can potentially be magnetically polarized and resonated, but only electrons really respond to the relatively slow movement of light waves.

Space can get really hot, but most of it is really cold. We're talking 10 to 30 degrees above absolute zero.

Elements floating around space can reach superconducting status at those temperatures. The Meissner effect is likely to be significant between objects made of these elements -- [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Meissner\_effect](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Meissner_effect)

Add that to the interactions between ionised plasma ....

### Jack Kruse — 2014-11-25T02:17:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-9-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12199/post-148365>

Photons are the force carrier for the electromagnetic force.......Where does light come from? Physics says protons. Magnets can exert a force at a distance, just like electric charges. Magnetic fields permeate space and you, and are strongest near a permanent magnet or electromagnet.

Things for you to ponder:

In a purely electric field, a magnetic field may be represented with field lines or flux lines. These lines, and the associated magnetic field, **point from the north pole of a magnet to the south pole of a magnet**. They are always closed flux lines. This is unlike electric field lines which are ALWAYS open. Since magnetic field lines are always closed, they never have a starting point or stopping point. Could this be why things with constrained magnetic fields are usually circular or an ellipse like a Star, planet, or a mitochondria? Whenever you have a north pole, you must have a south pole as well when discussing magnetic fields. This implies in magnetic field generation we never see single magnetic poles in nature. These are called magnetic monopoles, and so far physics do not think they exist. Contrast this with electric monopoles. They are present everywhere in nature in abundance. Examples are an electron, a proton, or any other charged particle. Interesting huh?

Magnetism: Permanent magnets dont equal magnetic fields created in space that vary because of how matter accumulates.
Because of this, permanent magnets are not the only objects which experience the magnetic force. Electric charges can experience a magnetic force if two conditions are met:

1.  The charge must be moving through a magnetic field
2.  The velocity of the charge cannot be parallel (or antiparallel) to the direction of the magnetic field.

As atomic physics and chemistry began to explain the periodic table with the help of the Bohr model of the atom in the early 1900s, magnetic properties were arbitrarily assigned to the electrons in atoms. Electrons appeared to exhibit two types of motion in an atom: orbital and spin. Orbital motion referred to the motion of an electron around the nucleus of the atom. Since a charged particle was moving, a magnetic field was created. Here is the irony though, protons also spin around their axis and induce another magnetic field............but people forget this one.......and your mitochondria are loaded with H+

In solid state physics, magnetism and vibration can be coupled in something called a magnetoelastic wave. This is something called phonon-magnon coupling. It has to do with the relationship between magnetic spin and vibration in the organized crystal lattice of a magnetic material.

So I would guess that certain kinds of vibrations might be capable of enhancing magnetic activity through application of this mechanism in crystalline biologic materials, solid or liquid. ### Josh — 2014-11-25T05:35:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-9-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12199/post-148385>

> **Spontaneous magnetization** is the appearance of an ordered [spin](http://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spin_\(physics\)) state ([magnetization](http://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Magnetization)) at zero applied magnetic field in a[ferromagnetic](http://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ferromagnetic) or [ferrimagnetic](http://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ferrimagnetic) material below a [critical point](http://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Critical_point_\(physics\)) called the [Curie temperature](http://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Curie_temperature) or TC....
> Heated to temperatures above TC, ferromagnetic materials become [paramagnetic](http://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Paramagnetic) and their magnetic behavior is dominated by [spin waves](http://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spin_wave) or [magnons](http://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Magnon), which are [boson](http://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Boson) collective excitations with energies in the meV range. The magnetization that occurs below TC is a famous example of the ["spontaneous" breaking](http://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spontaneous_symmetry_breaking) of a global symmetry, a phenomenon that is described by [Goldstone's theorem](http://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Goldstone%27s_theorem). The term "symmetry breaking" refers to the choice of a magnetization direction by the spins, which have spherical symmetry above TC, but a preferred axis (the magnetization direction) below TC.
>
> **Temperature dependence[Edit](http://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spontaneous_magnetization#/editor/2)**
> To first order, the temperature dependence of spontaneous magnetization at low temperatures is given by [Bloch's](http://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Felix_Bloch) Law:[\[1\]](http://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spontaneous_magnetization#cite_note-1)
>
> { "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }
>
>
> where M(0) is the spontaneous magnetization at absolute zero. The decrease in spontaneous magnetization at higher temperatures is caused by the increasing excitation of spin waves. In a particle description, the spin waves correspond to magnons, which are the massless [Goldstone bosons](http://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Goldstone_boson) corresponding to the [broken symmetry](http://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Continuous_symmetry). This is exactly true for an isotropic magnet.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-11-25T06:12:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-9-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12199/post-148389>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=148382)
>
> You are gonna be hearing about vibrations and oscillations soon

**Still Stuck on Understanding Enzymes**

Excerpt from blog:

_The hydrogen bonding network in water is a quantum measuring device because its motions and action provides a record or a memory of the state of entangled pair of electrons in free radicals made in mitochondria._ This is why mitochondria use free radicals to signal.

They also release _monochromatic infrared light_ because you can only **entangle electrons** and protons with light at **one specific frequency**. The double slit experiment proving quantum entanglement only works if light is monochromatic.

When free radicals are formed at the cytochromes with “like spinning electrons” become entangled by light or magnetic fields these particles can hit water molecules by themselves or as a pair, the subsequent atomic motions in water will be affected by the state of the particle that hits water. These two electrons will also act in unison instantaneously no matter the distance put between them.

Entangled particles brought together have their spins balanced but when one spins counterclockwise, the other instantaneously spins clockwise.​

My current approach has been to look into a simple enzyme and free-radical pair: Superoxide and Superoxide Dismutaste (SOD). I picked that pair because O2- is pretty strongly charged, gets produced everywhere, and somehow or rather a little bit of SOD must be able to instantly act as a "lightning rod" (literally) to draw these O2- radicals directly to it.

SOD1 in the cytoplasm needs zinc and copper to work, while SOD2 in the mitochondria need Manganese to work.

Quoting wikipedia ([http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Superoxide\_dismutase](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Superoxide_dismutase)), reactions are:

This SOD-catalyzed [dismutation](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dismutation) of [superoxide](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Superoxide) may be written, for Cu,Zn SOD, with the following half-reactions​

-   -   Cu2+-SOD + O2− → Cu+-SOD + O2
    -   Cu+-SOD + O2− + 2H+ → Cu2+-SOD + H2O2

The general form, applicable to all the different metal-coordinated forms of SOD, can be written as follows:​

-   -   M(n+1)+-SOD + O2− → Mn+-SOD + O2
    -   Mn+-SOD + O2− + 2H+ → M(n+1)+-SOD + H2O2.

where M = [Cu](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Copper) (n=1) ; [Mn](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manganese) (n=2) ; [Fe](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Iron) (n=2) ; [Ni](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nickel) (n=2).​

I'd like to highlight that the transition metal first needs to **take away an electron** from a superoxide radical to make O2, and then needs **to give away an electron** to the superoxide radical in the presence of two H+ ions (important!).

Again, the Nickel-based SOD are not used in eukaryotes. No clue why

Someone needs to correct me on this, but I know superoxide + water is highly exothermic. Though I'm not whether the reactions above are, I would suspect they are. This leads into the following question ...

**Question**: ordinarily I would think that superoxide is highly reactive, and there would be no "activation energy" needed for any sort of reaction. This mention about spin-wave interactions, as well as SOD's unique affinity to superoxide, makes it feel like superoxide is seeking out the most energetically favourable method to react with another compound, and the SODs happen to have the correct spin and EM absorption/emission characteristics to do so. Is this complete bullshit, or somewhat on base?

### DavidL — 2014-11-25T06:32:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-9-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12199/post-148390>

One last thing on pyramids: So here's some interesting information I have learned from watching ~everyone's favorite~ David Wilcock's Wisodom Teachings regarding how he thinks the pyramids may have been built. Dr. Nikolai Kozyrev did an experiment in his basement where he dropped a cannonball on his floor. Dr. Kozyrev weighed the ball before and after dropping the ball and he astoundingly found that the ball weighed less after it was dropped.

The weight noticably decreases after the drop. Over 20 minutes the weight comes back in intervals. In sudden interval jumps.

What David is saying is that the atoms in the cannonball are losing their mass and are not subject to gravitational fields.

He theorizes that the atoms are missing because they slam into the floor and become a wave, not a particle, thus losing mass.

Sound waves can cause particles to oscillate about a mean position - if those particles are crossing lines of magnetic flux as they osclillate they can induce an electric current. This current would alternate unless passing through the biological equivalent of a diode or wheatstone bridge. Microphones which convert sound into electrical signals can alternatively be called linear generators. > [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=148375)
>
> As atomic physics and chemistry began to explain the periodic table with the help of the Bohr model of the atom in the early 1900s, magnetic properties were arbitrarily assigned to the electrons in atoms. Electrons appeared to exhibit two types of motion in an atom: orbital and spin. Orbital motion referred to the motion of an electron around the nucleus of the atom. Since a charged particle was moving, a magnetic field was created. Here is the irony though, protons also spin around their axis and induce another magnetic field............but people forget this one.......and your mitochondria are loaded with H+

Hmmm . . . . I havent pinned it down yet but this causes a property of electrical induction motors to pop into my head called "back emf" - it is the property whereby an electric motor also generates an opposing emf to the one driving it. It is what determines the unloaded speed of the motor and also explains why an electric motor generates maximum torque at stall speed. This would imply that anything acting to slow down the atomic orbits or spins causes an imbalance and creates an emf. Probably way off track but its just what popped into my head.

> [nonchalant said:](/goto/post?id=148450)
>
> I sometimes wonder about PD tremors. Can the movements be helpful?

Perhaps. A lot of people don't realize that the major clinical problem in parkinsonism is the "lack of movement," not the tremor. See below...

[http://www.medscape.com/viewarticle/775696\_12](http://www.medscape.com/viewarticle/775696_12)

> resting tremor may emerge as a collateral effect of cerebral mechanisms that compensate for pathophysiological changes producing akinesia (Hallett and Khoshbin, 1980; Rivlin-Etzion _et al_., 2006)

> there is post-mortem evidence that patients with non-tremor Parkinson's disease have more cortical lesions than patients with tremor-dominant Parkinson's disease, and this may explain the worse cognitive dysfunction of patients with non-tremor Parkinson's disease

### yewwei.tan — 2014-11-25T22:20:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-9-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12199/post-148472>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=148423)
>
> I love that you get this.............This is how piezoelectric currents can be reversed naturally. It is a bidirectional pathway.

Others things that you said on the previous page of this thread:

ionized plasma has bidirectional flow in space........electric currents inside the ionosphere are unidirectional.

Electric charges can experience a magnetic force if two conditions are met:

1.  The charge must be moving through a magnetic field
2.  The velocity of the charge cannot be parallel (or antiparallel) to the direction of the magnetic field.

From the link that @[Da-mo](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/9712/) shared: [http://www.tensegrityinbiology.co.uk/biology.html](http://www.tensegrityinbiology.co.uk/biology.html) (great read BTW, everyone should take a look. I know I missed a lot of points made on that page)

Helixes were mentioned to: _behave as tensegrity structures in their own right as they naturally stabilize through a balance between the forces of attraction (tension) and repulsion (compression)_

Firstly, it sounds that bidirectional flow through helical networks (like bone and the collagen in tendons) occur simultaneously. The network is actually the strongest when forces of attraction and repulsion are equally strong

Secondly, the helical structure always meets the 2 conditions quoted above for a charged particle/wave to experience a magnetic force.

I would also think that the helix is self constricting:

With that said:

-   What are the charge carriers? Electrons and Protons sound like the 2 candidates, but what else? What is the right ratio?

-   How do create more of them? Oscillations around hydrophilic proteins generates more EZ water, and now we have these effects of oscillations (sound) on magnetism. Kinetic distortions of the network translate directly into induced magnetic moments and thus electric charges as well.

-   How do protons carry charge? Proton spin cannot be derived from its components quarks, neither can its rest mass. "Rest mass" may not even make sense for a proton, because it essentially is a store of kinetic energy (of the constituent particles of the proton) which must manifest itself on various interactions.

    A proton's spin is commonly thought of to be a constant integer, but what are the implications if direct modification of the proton's "stored kinetic energy" also allows for subsequent quantised changes in spin? Half-spin protons sound very useful for lots of things (and may be a solution to enzyme mechanics)

    This is also where the point made by @[NeilBB](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/6879/) about distinguishing matter from mass becomes important. Proton mass is basically energy, but I don't know what that energy (and thus the proton's mass) is dependent on.

    See "Proton Spin Crisis" -- [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Proton\_spin\_crisis](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Proton_spin_crisis)

Sound waves can be used to tranfer heat (IR light) along a material and via small oscillations in pressure cause a temperature gradient.

How does this tie in with the actin and integrin networks of the body?

>
> I like the idea Neil (quoted above) about this possibly explaining the strong force.
>
> Should also note that the Carbon atom nucleus is considered a boson, and that bosons with the same energy try to occupy the same space.
>
> Cooling has the effect of condensing bosons in the sense that if you reduce the number of possible energetic states of the bosons, then they will statistically be more bosons with the same energy, and naturally these bosons will try to occupy the same space. This condensation does actually result in a small amount of actual volume condensation, but it mostly refers to a reduction in the number of energetic quanta that a boson can occupy.
>
> How does this affect the distribution of charges? Boson crystal latticess? An even number fermions (half-spin) can form a boson (integer spin), so what of the transition metals which seem to mediate all these quantum mechanical effects -- Josephson junctions, SQUIDs, Cooper pairs, etc ....? I have no clue yet
>
> Sidenote: I had recently re-read the Quantum Cell blog post, and it seems very relevant to these topics -- [http://jackkruse.com/ee-6-quantum-cell-theory-life-collective-phenomena/](http://jackkruse.com/ee-6-quantum-cell-theory-life-collective-phenomena/)
>

Fun facts:
Did you know that even though a neutron has no "overall charge," it has been found to have sub-charged regional distribution, with a negative "skin," positive "middle" and negative "core?"
And although it has no net electric charge, because of this charge distribution and its geometry, it does have a net "magnetic moment." A magnetic moment that resembles that of a negatively charged particle.
That means that although neutrons cannot be manipulated by electrical fields, they can be influenced by magnetic fields.
It is basically a subatomic particle that is magnetic but not electric.
And neutrons are of course, integral in the construction of all atomic nuclei except the rogue hydrogen.
I find that extremely interesting...

### NeilBB — 2014-11-26T01:24:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-9-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12199/post-148500>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=148367)
>
> ionized plasma has bidirectional flow in space........electric currents inside the ionosphere are unidirectional.

Well, there is something called a "double layer" in a plasma. Magnetohydrodynamics is the field...
Basically, Maxwell's equations have to be solved simultaneously with the differential equations of fluid dynamics to fully decscribe electromagnetism in a plasma...
You get the Vlasov–Maxwell system of equations... Mind-numbingly complicated stuff...

### Da-mo — 2014-11-26T01:30:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-9-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12199/post-148503>

[http://www.oocities.org/rjwarren\_stm/Physics\_Notes/U4\_Electro.html](http://www.oocities.org/rjwarren_stm/Physics_Notes/U4_Electro.html)

> **Figure 14.1. Measurement of the magnetic susceptibility in an inhomogeneous magnetic field. The electromagnet is driven by an electric current, which flows through the helical windings of a long insulated wire called a solenoid. The magnetic flux lines (dashed) follow the iron core. [http://what-when-how.com/electronic-properties-of-materials/foundations-of-magnetism-magnetic-properties-of-materials/](http://what-when-how.com/electronic-properties-of-materials/foundations-of-magnetism-magnetic-properties-of-materials/[QUOTE)**[\[QUOTE](http://what-when-how.com/electronic-properties-of-materials/foundations-of-magnetism-magnetic-properties-of-materials/[QUOTE)\]

> The tubular nature of the helix scales up into blood vessels . . . . .
> In the walls of elastic arteries, such as the aorta, helical collagen reinforcement resists high loads from the pressure of blood. The middle layer organizes into lamellar units, with the orientation of collagen fibres and smooth muscle cells forming a continuous helix. Collagen is more dispersed in the outer adventitia, but still forms two helical groups of fibres . . . .
> **5.1 Helical tubes**
>
> The formation of capillaries results from tension-dependent interactions between endothelial cells and an extra-cellular scaffold of their own construction, and is described through tensegrity.86 The growing matrix causes changes in the configuration of cytoskeletal components,5 and initiates chemical signalling cascades that influence further development of the capillary network.87
> The capacity for fluid flow through a tube depends, in part, on the porosity of the tube wall. The helical tensegrity ‘wall’ in figure 8 has many gaps, but if the struts were expanded into plates that just touched each other, they could be made to ‘seal’ the internal space. This compares with the selective barrier of endothelial cells that allows vascular contents to pass out between capillary walls. The internal cellular cytoskeleton determines cell shape and orientation, through tensegrity;5 is affected by signalling mechanisms and variations in fluid flow; and alters the tension between cells through adherens junctions,88 ultimately affecting tube permeability.89,90[http://www.tensegrityinbiology.co.uk/articles/helix.html](http://www.tensegrityinbiology.co.uk/articles/helix.html)

-   So it seems that our circulatory systems are made from helical tubes that might generate magnetic feilds as in the top figure.
-   In the second figure, an iron core passing through the helical tube is used to direct magnetic flux. Is this analogous to paramagnetic material in our circulatory fluids (blood)?
-   If the above two points hold - could the gap in the magnet in the 2nd figure be analagous to the tissues in between the arterial and veinous networks?
-   The last figure and caption alludes to the wall permeability of a helical tube - and rather than thinking of plates as decribed - maybe thinking of how a helical tube which is rotated and shortend will close the gaps in the walls could indicates a variable permeability in biological helical structures . . . . vasoconstriction? muscular contraction?

The last point causes me to wonder about the rendency for stress or pain to cause tense muscles - could this be a natural way of reducing permeability in order to keep stressors out of the tissue? Or is it an effect of a loss of magnetism somewhere in the structure?

Do stressors always increase tension or destroy magnetism?
Do magnets applied to tight muscles relax them?
Thinking of the opposite here with the Salamander that was anaesthetised with magnetism.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-11-26T02:34:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-9-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12199/post-148516>

> [NeilBB said:](/goto/post?id=148500)
>
> Well, there is something called a "double layer" in a plasma. Magnetohydrodynamics is the field...
> Basically, Maxwell's equations have to be solved simultaneously with the differential equations of fluid dynamics to fully decscribe electromagnetism in a plasma...
> You get the Vlasov–Maxwell system of equations... Mind-numbingly complicated stuff...

There is key at the heart of QED and magnetism that has confused physicists for a long time. The Aharonov-Bohm effect showed for the first time that a magnetic field inside a confined region can have a measureable impact on a charged particle which never traveled inside the region. Now think about a mitochondria. Does it confine a magnetic field? This AB action is purely a quantum effect, verified in a multitude of experiments. It is at the heart of modern physics. In 1986, Akira Tonomura and his coworkers used electron holography to empirically prove the existence of the Aharonov-Bohm effect. I wrote about this once before.........somewhere.

In theory, every atom or molecule has the potential to be magnetic, since this depends on the movement of its electrons. But could it depend upon other things. Electrons move in two ways: spin, which can loosely be thought as spinning around themselves like tops, and orbits, which refers to an electron's movement around the nucleus of its atom. The spin and orbital motion gives rise to the magnetization, similar to an electric current circulating in a coil and producing a magnetic field. The spinning direction of the electrons therefore defines the direction of the magnetization in a material.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-11-26T02:52:06+0200

Suggestions that other forces including gravity may travel almost infinite distances more or less instantaneously has got me wondering how this affects the body.

Light has a universal speed limit at 186,000 miles an hour. Light travels 30 centimeters in one nanosecond.

GPS clocks orbiting the Earth must run faster than clocks on the ground to navigate properly. They are 38 microseconds faster. In 38 microseconds at the speed of light, if this clock difference did not exist, GPS devices would be off by over ten kilometers a day.

As we raise from earth what happens to these forces? Gravity falls off and the electromagnetic forces increase while time slows..............interesting huh?

Just listened to the ePhysiologix podcast with Dr K on CT while riding my bike 12km to Ellis Beach  -- [http://www.ephysiologix.com/living-the-optimized-life-episode-33-jack-kruse-cold-thermogenesis/](http://www.ephysiologix.com/living-the-optimized-life-episode-33-jack-kruse-cold-thermogenesis/)

Probably didn't listen to it very well, and will listen to it again tomorrow, but a couple of points stood out. I feel this thread actually covered a lot of the points. The 2 points that did stand out were:

-   Need to start studying European Robin magnetic sense
-   Need to read the paper I mentioned somewhere before '**Atomic Description of an Enzyme Reaction Dominated by Proton Tunneling**' -- [http://www.sciencemag.org/content/312/5771/237.full](http://www.sciencemag.org/content/312/5771/237.full) . Jack mentioned proton KIE in the podcast -- [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kinetic\_isotope\_effect](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kinetic_isotope_effect) , knowing this will probably be helpful again when reading the next blog

Switching between H2S and NO chemistry was also talked about. I wonder how well I'm going to handle the cold in Tokyo this Sunday, after having spent 7 weeks in the sun. Theoretically the switch should be instant, but we'll see. Decent temp range of 1-12C (39-59F), which should be decent enough CT if I'm wearing a t-shirt most of the day -- [http://www.weather.com/weather/tenday/l/JAXX0085:1:JA](http://www.weather.com/weather/tenday/l/JAXX0085:1:JA)

### NeilBB — 2014-11-26T05:32:35+0200

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=148525)
>
> You'll see.............

My last thought on this before I collapse... It has been controversially proposed that a version of cold fusion/LENR could actually involve not fusion of hydrogen to helium, but instead the transmutation of nickel into copper in the presence of hydrogen ions (protons), releasing a large amount of heat (IR light) in the process.

It seems to me that something like this could maybe happen inside the mitochondrial matrix. Especially given the metallic content of the membrane proteins and the existence of the local magnetic fields and electron current flow present to initiate such a process...

[http://www.extremetech.com/extreme/...ied-has-10000-times-the-energy-density-of-gas](http://www.extremetech.com/extreme/156393-cold-fusion-reactor-independently-verified-has-10000-times-the-energy-density-of-gas)

> Basically, hydrogen ions (single protons) are sucked into a nickel lattice; the nickel’s electrons are forced into the hydrogen to produce neutrons; the nickel nuclei absorb these neutrons; the neutrons are stripped of their electrons to become protons; and thus the nickel goes up in atomic number from 28 to 29, becoming copper.

And conveniently, the excess copper produced by this process would be able to then be utilized in the cytochrome proteins to tunnel more electrons...

So perhaps multiple different atomic transmutations are possible with "proton blasting." Maybe this happens in stars. This technology could potentially bail us out of the EMF-carbon nightmare. And, if it happens in mitochondria, maybe transmutations of different elements would release different net energy levels, and maybe different quantized packets of energy, or different frequencies of light?

### yewwei.tan — 2014-11-26T20:08:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-9-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12199/post-148580>

> [Da-mo said:](/goto/post?id=148543)
>
> @NeiBB your last few posts finally prompted me to read this: [http://electric-cosmos.org/Electronic Sun.pdf](<http://electric-cosmos.org/Electronic Sun.pdf>)
>
> Double layers of voltage gradients influencing proton flows, temperatures and velocities. Semiconductor analogies . . . . Dr. K has often compared mitochondria with stars.

Now I remember what surprised me in the podcast with Daneil Stickler ....

Jack mentioned that a photon from the Sun could take many thousands of years to reach us. This makes sense for a mitochondria to do, since you want them to be able to store energy and emit just the right amount of IR light to power life.

I had been aware of plasma double layer formations as means to accelerate particles naturally, but I have not seen the proponents of the EU model talk about how magnetic and electric fields constrain "potential photonic energy" within stars. If anyone is aware of that evidence, please let me know

Then again, I actually think that it is less likely that stars like our sun store energy at all, given the broad and continuous range of frequencies of the emitted light. Maybe some other stars do it, but then again, I would be looking for pronounced peaks, since I'd expect storage to require a storage element to absorb the light and store it (unless you literally can trap free photons in some way)

In mitochondria, the hints at protons as a storage form of IR light have been thrown around before. H3O+ is a great storage form for protons, and can be stored indefinitely. So I can definitely see how mitochondria could derive IR light from the kinetic energy of constituents of a proton. How a mitochondria does that? I'll wait for the next blog to drop some big ideas ....

Click [here](http://www.sciencedirect.com/science?_ob=GatewayURL&_origin=CELLPRESS&_urlversion=4&_method=citationSearch&_version=1&_piikey=S0960982212008093&md5=8762cb0a66ade6a3c0b99ced52c27696) to view the full text on ScienceDirect.
>
> **Highlights**
>
> -   Mitochondrial genomes harbor mutations affecting male, but not female, aging
> -   There appear to be numerous mutations in mitochondrial DNA affecting male aging
> -   Maternal inheritance of mitochondria thus has dramatic effects on male life history
> -   This process should generally contribute to sex differences in longevity and aging
>
> **Summary**
> The maternal transmission of mitochondrial genomes invokes a sex-specific selective sieve, whereby mutations in mitochondrial DNA can only respond to selection acting directly on females \[ 1–3 \]. In theory, this enables male-harming mutations to accumulate in mitochondrial genomes when these same mutations are neutral, beneficial, or only slightly deleterious in their effects on females \[ 1–3 \]. Ultimately, this evolutionary process could result in the evolution of male-specific mitochondrial mutation loads; an idea previously termed Mother’s Curse \[ 2, 4–6 \]. Here, we present evidence that the effects of this process are broader than hitherto realized, and that it has resulted in mutation loads affecting patterns of aging in male, but not female _Drosophila melanogaster_. Furthermore, our results indicate that the mitochondrial mutation loads affecting male aging generally comprise numerous mutations over multiple sites. Our findings thus suggest that males are subject to dramatic consequences that result from the maternal transmission of mitochondrial genomes. They implicate the diminutive mitochondrial genome as a hotspot for mutations that affect sex-specific patterns of aging, thus promoting the idea that a sex-specific selective sieve in mitochondrial genome evolution is a contributing factor to sexual dimorphism in aging, commonly observed across species \[ 7–9 \].

[http://www.cell.com/current-biology/abstract/S0960-9822(12)00809-3](http://www.cell.com/current-biology/abstract/S0960-9822%2812%2900809-3)

> **Mitochondria, maternal inheritance, and asymmetric fitness: Why males die younger**
>
> 1.  Jonci N. Wolff1,2 and
> 2.  Neil J. Gemmell3,4,\*
>
> Article first published online: 27 DEC 2012
>
>
> **Abstract**
> Mitochondrial function is achieved through the cooperative interaction of two genomes: one nuclear (nuDNA) and the other mitochondrial (mtDNA). The unusual transmission of mtDNA, predominantly maternal without recombination is predicted to affect the fitness of male offspring. Recent research suggests the strong sexual dimorphism in aging is one such fitness consequence. The uniparental inheritance of mtDNA results in a selection asymmetry; mutations that affect only males will not respond to natural selection, imposing a male-specific mitochondrial mutation load. Prior work has implicated this male-specific mutation load in disease and infertility, but new data from fruit flies suggests a prominent role for mtDNA in aging; across many taxa males almost invariably live shorter lives than females. Here we discuss this new work and identify some areas of future research that might now be encouraged to explore what may be the underpinning cause of the strong sexual dimorphism in aging.

[http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/bies.201200141/abstract](http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/bies.201200141/abstract)

This video was the big review of inheritance and such, 30:22 explains some of the variability in ketosis and CT from person to person. I know this is a bit of a right turn in terms of where we have been going, yet I was contemplating the subject of inheritance through mitochondrial DNA and wondering "why" some things are preserved and I guess this scales into "how" which is more or less related......

> Energy, ageing, fidelity and sex: oocyte
> mitochondrial DNA as a protected
> genetic template
> Wilson B. M. de Paula1
> , Cathy H. Lucas2
> , Ahmed-Noor A. Agip1
> ,
> Gema Vizcay-Barrena3 and John F. Allen1,4
> 1
> School of Biological and Chemical Sciences, Queen Mary University of London, Mile End Road,
> London E1 4NS, UK
> 2
> Ocean and Earth Science, National Oceanography Centre Southampton, University of Southampton,
> European Way, Southampton SO14 3ZH, UK
> 3
> Centre for Ultrastructural Imaging, King’s College London, New Hunt’s House Guy’s Campus,
> London SE1 1UL, UK
> 4
> Research Department of Genetics, Evolution and Environment, University College London, Gower Street,
> London WC1E 6BT, UK
> Oxidative phosphorylation couples ATP synthesis to respiratory electron
> transport. In eukaryotes, this coupling occurs in mitochondria, which
> carry DNA. Respiratory electron transport in the presence of molecular
> oxygen generates free radicals, reactive oxygen species (ROS), which are
> mutagenic. In animals, mutational damage to mitochondrial DNA therefore
> accumulates within the lifespan of the individual. Fertilization generally
> requires motility of one gamete, and motility requires ATP. It has been proposed
> that oxidative phosphorylation is nevertheless absent in the special
> case of quiescent, template mitochondria, that these remain sequestered in
> oocytes and female germ lines and that oocyte mitochondrial DNA is thus
> protected from damage, but evidence to support that view has hitherto
> been lacking. Here we show that female gametes of Aurelia aurita, the
> common jellyfish, do not transcribe mitochondrial DNA, lack electron
> transport, and produce no free radicals. In contrast, male gametes actively
> transcribe mitochondrial genes for respiratory chain components and produce
> ROS. Electron microscopy shows that this functional division of labour
> between sperm and egg is accompanied by contrasting mitochondrial morphology.
> We suggest that mitochondrial anisogamy underlies division of
> any animal species into two sexes with complementary roles in sexual reproduction.
> We predict that quiescent oocyte mitochondria contain DNA as an
> unexpressed template that avoids mutational accumulation by being transmitted
> through the female germ line. The active descendants of oocyte
> mitochondria perform oxidative phosphorylation in somatic cells and in
> male gametes of each new generation, and the mutations that they accumulated
> are not inherited. We propose that the avoidance of ROS-dependent
> mutation is the evolutionary pressure underlying maternal mitochondrial
> inheritance and the developmental origin of the female germ line.

[http://rstb.royalsocietypublishing.org/content/royptb/368/1622/20120263.full.pdf](http://rstb.royalsocietypublishing.org/content/royptb/368/1622/20120263.full.pdf)

### Josh — 2014-11-27T00:45:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-9-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12199/post-148607>

[http://www.mammag.uci.edu/philosophical.asp](http://www.mammag.uci.edu/philosophical.asp)

**Two Organisms Make a Man**

Humans are limited by their individual 'I' perspective, which is singular. Yet we know that each person is made of 30 trillion individuals called cells. Hence the 'I' is really a 'we'. This idea is also myopic, however, for we now know that each nucleated (eukaryotic) cell is composed of two different life forms that formed a symbiosis three to four billion years ago. Today, these two cells are represented by the nuclear-cytosol “host” cell and a colony of cytosolic bacteria called “mitochondria.” Thus, inside each of the 'we's is a 'they' (Figure 3).

**Figure 3: The Cellular Structures of the Two Human Organism and Their Symbiotic Relationship.** The left panel shows the relative physical relationship of the nucleus-cytosol and the mitochondria. The right upper panel shows a high resolution picture of the mitochondrial organism and the right lower panel shows electron micrographs of the mitochondrial DNA (mtDNA).

These two organisms were co-equal at the time of the original symbiosis. However, over the intervening billions of years they have become specialized. The nuclear-cytosol organism specialized in generating the structures of the cells and later of the tissues and organs (Figure 2, left half). The mitochondria specialized in energy generation to fuel the development of these increasingly complex structures, yet they still retain their own genomic integrity and separate information storage and retrieval systems (Figure 2, right half).

### NeilBB — 2014-12-04T16:44:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-9-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12199/post-149219>

> [Dali Dula said:](/goto/post?id=149214)
>
> The job of evolution is to endlessly reproduce. Everything else is just window dressing.

Thats a Darwinian interpretation--I'm not so sure about that. I think reproduction is just a by-product, a form of recycling of biomatter, like autophagy or cellular repair within a single organism.

I think the job of evolution is to locally contain entropy and to statistically battle the 2nd law of thermodynamics. I think if it can do that without reproduction, it would be perfectly content. And therefore, I'd conjecture that immortality is theoretically possible.

### NeilBB — 2014-12-04T18:39:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-9-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12199/post-149233>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=149221)
>
> Neill opens the can worms. It is the Aubrey de Grey idea. One I struggle with. I think life might innovate without reproduction but I think it would limit bio diversity and possible outcomes. Don't be afraid to ask stupid questions or question convention. The wise can learn more from a foolish question than a fool can learn from a wise answer. For example, you can't accomplish much by swimming with the mainstream. Even a dead fish can do that.
>

Well, I think reproduction is one of evolution's most powerful tools, but it's just that it may not actually be fundamental or essential. Biology views reproduction as the "sine qua non" of evolution. Jack, I think your theory of evolution as an "organization of energy combatting entropy" implies something different. Autophagy, speciation, individuation, reproduction, etc could all be viewed as really just tools in this process.

Reproduction provides a recombination of nuclear DNA which is clearly an adaptative advantage. Mitochondrial DNA provides stability without recombination, so eukaryotes have innovated the best of both worlds in this weird way...

Some primitive species are very long lived and reproduce less. I don't think "evolution" cares much about longevity of individuals or about reproduction per se, except insofar as these processes maintain "life processes," which by their nature organize energy and reduce entropy in a system. That seems to be basically what the universe wants to do, right? Organize and harness energy, however it can. That's looking at it from a physics perspective, not a biological one...

> This would suggest longevity, but makes me wonder the reason for andropause.

I think the reason for "andropause" is the modern world. I doubt seriously that it really existed before we invented nn-EMF, chronobiological and environmental mismatches, estrogenic chemicals in food, low-fat diets, statins, etc, etc, etc...

### NeilBB — 2014-12-05T00:03:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-9-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12199/post-149249>

> [Dali Dula said:](/goto/post?id=149247)
>
> There is no evolution without reproduction. The process of evolution requires reproduction. This just might be one of the self evident truths we have heard about. You may not believe in evolution, but if you do it ain't happening without birth and death at some level.

What I'm suggesting is that if evolution at its core is primarily a thermodynamic process of physics and cosmology, then evolution may have preceded not only the process of biological reproduction, but all of biology and life itself. And if it predated reproduction, it means reproduction was an innovation in the process, and therefore could NOT be an essential defining characteristic.

It is at least theoretically conceivable that an organism that developed robust-enough "epigenetic adaptation" abilities and also developed massive lifespan through really advanced cellular repair mechanisms could also counteract entropy in the same way. Nature thusfar has chosen disposable individuals within permanent (or semi-permanent) species, but I don't see that it would have to necessarily be that way... And sure, it is much easier to replace your iPhone every couple of years with a fresh, new improved one, but it is at least theoretically possible to keep repairing that same phone's parts over and over (and even upgrading certain parts) instead and keep the same phone running and harnessing energy indefinitely.

Far-fetched? Yeah, sure. But so is reality, when you think about it...

### Shijin13 — 2014-12-10T23:45:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-9-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12199/post-149756>

Damn.. I just spent the past several hours reading this.

really good stuff in here. Monday on a walk from the Pentagon to where I parked my car (Had a meeting in there - not back permanently!) My coworker asked me if I'd rather catch the metro since I didn't have a coat. my response of no - cold is good for you blew him away. Mind you... this discussion occurred after I had finished Tens9,10 & 11 and I'd been thinking about the whole anesthesia thing with regards to pulsed EMF - particularly beckers work with the salamander.

so back to the cold. I explained why cold makes you warmer through IR heat release at the mitochondria. I then explained the black hole/sun/super nova comparaision where when you sleep you want your mitochondria to be the black hole, and when you're awake you want them to be the sun, but if you're diseased the mitochondria become the supernova and eventually the cell dies, or you get cancer, and eventually death of the organism.

Now, I knew my co-worker is very religious/spiritual man. So I asked him the question - that by the way no one here on the board has asked - If we're created by God, and he created the universe and everything in it, and it's perfect - wouldn't everything be an echo of everything else in its design and function? He actually stopped walking and looked at me as if I'd struck him over the head (Wiley Coyote style) and asked WHAT? So I asked him the question again, but differently. If God created the universe , and within the universe are stars and systems that work together, aren't we the remnants of the stars? His reply - If I'm God and I created the Universe, I'd only have one set of directions to make everything, and I'd never deviate from that due to both the simplicity and the complexity.

And from there he asked the question - what is the end game in understanding this little cosmos within us and embracing the cold? Understanding that I can heal myself, of medical issues I've been told by doctors that they couldn't help me. Imagine trusting your instincts such that you always know where you're going and that you will never error, you may fail but you will not error.

I will say all these magnetic moments are bringing back many of my engineering classes. Now on to catching up on the threads for 10 and 11, then the webinars & Q&A's

> [NeilBB said:](/goto/post?id=148313)
>
> Okay, so you are a philosophical skeptic, like Hume. We can't know if the sun will ever rise, right? That is your bias. I reject that kind of skepticism. I don't regard one's having contextual certainty as necessarily diagnostic of narcissism. On your terms, no one can ever be certain of anything. Your standard of epistemology is "doubt." Doubt doesn't build bridges. That view is self-refuting. Even David Hume didn't live by his skepticism, people said he was a nice regular guy...
>
> Inductive knowledge is possible. It is limited and contextual and subject to certain types of revision, but it is still possible. You have to start at the observational level and build abstractions by a specific process. That is my point.
>
> Skepticism is good to employ to a point. But you also have to know when it's time to draw a conclusion. I would maintain that when to be skeptical and when to be assertive are epistemological issues and that epistemology is the science of human knowledge and is actually neither arbitrary nor subject to random opinions. I can't possibly prove that to you in this thread, but it is the basis of my mental self-confidence that you now question...

Confidence is a two edged knife, ie essential but must be handled carefully..

Brother John

### Christos — 2015-01-17T11:42:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-9-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12199/post-153123>

Good paper for those interested in details for free radical entanglement in bird magnetic reception, @yewwei-tan might find it interesting:
[http://www.ks.uiuc.edu/Research/cryptochrome/](http://www.ks.uiuc.edu/Research/cryptochrome/)
Basically cryptochrome in the eyes of birds entangle FAD (blue light absorption moiety) and 02- (superoxide) for which the earths magnetic field can then change this radical pair into singlet or triplet states. The changing in singlet and triplet states may cause light variation in the birds visual system thus allowing it to "see" where to fly.
This is analogous to complex one in mitochondria which has has an FMN moiety that has an isolloxazine ring that is identical to FAD. NADH electrons bind to FAD of complex 1 and release blue light. Superoxide is also made at complex 1, hence forth mitochndria might entangle superoxide and FAD, thus allowing such radical pair to be responsive to mitochondrias magnetic feild.

Ha! The content of that site was actually posted in the forums in Feb-2014  -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.ph...nd-magnetic-sensing-of-circadian-cycles.9584/](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/cryptochromes-and-magnetic-sensing-of-circadian-cycles.9584/)

My <08-Jan-2015> forum post is also relevant -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.ph...ady-for-you-comments.12507/page-7#post-152040](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/tensegrity-13-is-live-and-ready-for-you-comments.12507/page-7#post-152040)

I'm going to be an immediate downer, and say that I think the action of Melanopsin ([http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Melanopsin](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Melanopsin)) is much more important than the action of Cryptochromes in mammals, and definitely in humans.

Sidenote: The Quantum Sleep blog went into the importance of Melanopsin for vortex reversal in the third ventricle needed for deep sleep -- [http://jackkruse.com/energy-epigenetics-9-quantum-sleep/](http://jackkruse.com/energy-epigenetics-9-quantum-sleep/)
​

Paper: '**Regulation of the Mammalian Circadian Clock by Cryptochrome**' -- [http://www.iovs.org/content/44/10/4515.long](http://www.iovs.org/content/44/10/4515.long)

Mice of the _rd_/_rd_;_Cry1_–/–; _Cry2_–_/_– genotype, which have no classical opsins or cryptochromes but retain melanopsin, exhibit nearly 3000-fold reduction in circadian photosensitivity

At face value, the retinal depletion data show that cryptochrome can mediate circadian photoreception in the eye in the absence of functional opsins. However, this conclusion is at odds with the finding that elimination of opsins by genetic ablation abolishes all visual and non-visual photoresponses.

Clearly, in the absence of opsins, cryptochrome cannot generate an action potential for neurotransmission from the retina to the SCN​

I want to say that Melanopsin is an upgraded Rhodopsin giving "the best of all worlds":

-   performing the same action as rhodopsin if need be
-   itself being sensitive to the 300-500nm wavelengths that the Cryptochromes are (and being present where the cryptochromes are)
-   G-protein coupled receptors to talk with other signalling molecules from the rest of the body (like the gut -- Tensegrity #14 and Jan Webinar alert!)

Cryptochromes also require to be light-activated. It is the simultaneous presence of blue-light, and an external magnetic field, that causes variation in CRY responses and allows navigation in birds.

I want to say that Melanopsin, by virtue of more inputs from the rest of the body, can be magnetic sensitive without the need for any light input at all, and can then relay this information to the SCN. Don't know the details though

Sidenote: Some random details here -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.ph...about-infrared-and-ct.9675/page-4#post-146856](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/a-little-story-about-infrared-and-ct.9675/page-4#post-146856))

Another relevant paper '**Expression of the Blue-Light Receptor Cryptochrome in the Human Retina**' -- [http://www.iovs.org/content/44/10/4515.long](http://www.iovs.org/content/44/10/4515.long)​

Hehe, nobody is assuming things here , and nobody is trying to put words in your mouth .

The purpose of my last post in this thread was to ask if Melanopsin performs a superset of the functionality of Cryptochromes, and to ascertain the appropriate failure cascades. eg: Does a failure in CRY cause a failure in Melanopsin signalling? Does a failure in Melanopsin cause a failure in CRY signalling?

IMO, right now this is not an important question to answer, since we don't have the ability to engineer Melanopsin and Cryptochrome signalling within the retina. The only thing that we can control is the environmental input, and it's clear that screwing with either Melanopsin or Cryptochrome function is bad enough to the system as a whole.

The only relevance to my question is in some future whereby we actually can do something like alter or replace components of the retina . Then I guess we can focus research and practical efforts on fixing the thing with the best bang-for-your-buck.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-01-20T06:47:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-9-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12199/post-153408>

^^^^^And there is another loss.........of magnetic sense on cell membranes we have yet to probe. Topologic insulators are inherently chaotic on their surface and naturally show extreme disorder of the surface electrons on cell membranes. In plasma research individual electrons act as massless electrons, but groups of these electrons form a quasi-like crystalline structure on the surface that is chaotic but has a definite mass. This is known as the "Dirac mass." The mass of a sub atomic particles are a measure of the energy is absorbed in orbital deformation of that particle. This Dirac mass of cell membranes is interesting for biophysics because of how it can interact with magnetic fields.

Dirac mass results from surface particles' interactions with magnetic fields. The environment’s magnetic fields can impart order on the chaos at the surface. These fields are created by the presence of magnetic atoms substituted into the cell or membranes crystal lattice to convert it into a ferromagnetic topological insulator. The magnetic chaos eventually destroys the normal exotic surface state.......................

Now consider the implications of non native EMF signals to the Dirac mass built into cell membranes. It can affect electrons or protons and alter their masses in a big way to alter their abilities to transfer energy or information. In fact, the effects of pulsed non native irradiation may also be transmitted chemically in the blood plasma. It affects the ion balance on colloidal particles suspended in the plasma (why TG rise in non native exposures) such as those in blood plasma, giving them the ability to remove calcium ions from surfaces of arteries and cells and can make cell go from hydrophilic to hydrophobic. Non native EMF exposures also dehydrates cells, so it makes sense a cell would become more hydrophobic as you get dehydrated. Hydrophobicity means the cell has less energy within it, therefore its orbital circumference changes and it enlarges. The effect on intracellular water is to lower the size of the EZ and the result is a higher blood pressure because the PVN is stimulated abnormally. This is the real mechanism involved in the generation of adrenal fatigue.

(Took a walk on the Beach and made these speculations. I usually think like a crazy man on the beach , so don't expect the following to have any formal basis of proof)
\----

Now you're hinting at the "How" of the magnetic effects on cell membranes. So far, the observed effects I've seen in the literature still seem to be tied to Ca2+ levels inside and outside the cell -- [http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/bem.10136/abstract](http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/bem.10136/abstract)

More interesting is some of the stuff I skimmed in Mae-Wan Ho's 'Living Rainbow H2O', which describes water's quasicrystal structure in Chapter 4, and how different sphingolipids exposed on a cell membrane affect the number of levels of symmetry in the interfacial water quasi-crystal, which would thereby alter it's electromagnetic resonant effects.

I'm not sure if magnetostriction applies to the outer cell membrane -- [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Magnetostriction](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Magnetostriction) . It's an intrinsic property of ferromagnetic materials, including ferrofluids, and I haven't seen data about this, but also wouldn't be surprised if charge separated water managed to hold ferromagnetic ions and act as a ferrofluid.

A magnetostrive effect on the cell membrane would affect it's resonant frequencies.

\----

Which actually brings us to the more important question: Why does a cell need to sense magnetism?

I've been looking to definite what "magnetic sense" really means. So far I've come down to:

-   The cell can receive magnetic information about the environment and mount an appropriate response
-   The cell can use resonant magnetic waves to derive energy (magnetoelastic behaviour)
-   The cell can produce harmonics of it's resonant magnetic waves to communicate with other cells

Sidenote: I'm using the word "cell" here, but TBH, given the fractal nature of energetic transmission, this really refers to interactions between any bodies of any size which can interact via resonant effects.

If those 3 points are true, then it may imply that certain frequencies of nn-EMF are not as harmful.

In other words, harmonics of nn-EMF that are low-powered enough, and which do not correspond to any harmonic of any base frequency used by the cell, may actually not affect cellular processes to a large degree. (The only relevance here that I will think of is to design less harmful nn-EMF systems.)

Of course, I personally don't think that given the number of resonant molecules in the body (just think of how many types of enzymes there are!), that there is no such thing as an "unused spectrum" of EMF for which safe nn-EMF can be used.

\----

Finally, based on the above properties, I think that nn-EMF, which propagates at a higher-powered harmonic multiplier of a particular cells base resonant frequency, will easily:

-   over-amplify the signal and over-excite any receivers
-   cause greater distortions in orbital structures of resonant receivers and (by definition) increase their mass
-   therefore force transmitters to use more energetic harmonics for the tasks mentioned above

...

### Jack Kruse — 2015-01-20T15:44:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-9-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12199/post-153434>

Why does a cell need to sense magnetism? It organizes chaos...........the surface or TI's are inherently built chaotic.......and the native field orders them to alter the Dirac mass. That sense is altered by light dark transitions. The physics of organisms is to create statistical order to the thermodynamics of life........life has to be on a ledge between thermodynamics and quantum processes. It is that transition that is problematic for modern biology because it is all quantized. All divalent ions are a sort of cell cement.........when you add non native oscillators is knocks them out of their membrane structure. It is akin to shaking a tree at different forces........low force generation does nothing........but as the shaking increases oscillations rise and more unripe fruit falls to the ground and causes the tree to alter its life cycle.........when that fruit hits the ground in winter no seed will germinate and the tree species may not survive.

### yewwei.tan — 2015-01-20T22:47:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-9-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12199/post-153461>

That sense is altered by light dark transitions. The physics of organisms is to create statistical order to the thermodynamics of life........life has to be on a ledge between thermodynamics and quantum processes. It is that transition that is problematic for modern biology because it is all quantized. All divalent ions are a sort of cell cement.........when you add non native oscillators is knocks them out of their membrane structure. It is akin to shaking a tree at different forces........low force generation does nothing........but as the shaking increases oscillations rise and more unripe fruit falls to the ground and causes the tree to alter its life cycle.........when that fruit hits the ground in winter no seed will germinate and the tree species may not survive.

"Divalent ions (like Ca2+) are a cell cement" is one perspective that I'll be taking when reading through 'Living Rainbow H2O' . I know Ling talked about this a lot, so it's probably time to look into the details.

Of course that still leaves unanswered the question about functional units of organisation, how they communicate, etc .... And yes, they are fractal, up to a certain point of organisation which is either "functional enough" or "too energy inefficient" to scale up further using the available physical substrate ..... In many ways that's where a new perspective on many signalling pathways that conventional biology has already discovered (hormones).

I'm all up for emergent units of organisation, but that makes it basically impossible to truly figure out failure cascades without actually causing the effects to happen. Eventually, we'd be able to walk the line of causality using simple rules in a computer system, but we're not there yet.

The tree analogy is funny in that

-   The branching and root organisation of a tree is a clear visualisation of a fractal system
-   The most similar context to the body is a bunch of trees in a rainforest (lots of water) with interlocked canopies => oscillations on one tree propagate to it's adjacent neighbours
-   Set fire to one tree and watch the entire canopy burn . Fell one tree and you bring down many others, and you also lose the oscillatory information transfer mechanism

Just saying that you can look at a real-life rainforest (I have one 3km from me ), look at some of it's failure modes, and say "that's how the body fails", and actually be right (fractals!) ..... Time to read 'The 6th Extinction'.

.....

Don't forget vibration (from sound or EM) induces mass transport.

Vibration/oscillation induced movement is a possible mechanism for two reactants otherwise in too dilute a cell environment to have any chance of randomly walking in to each other yet they move toward each other when their resonant vibration frequency is struck. If that resonant vibration is disturbed by frequency coupling from another source, they might not vibrate toward each other and miss a reaction opportunity. I don't think they have the whole cell volume to move through anyway since most of the cell volume is taken by structured water. These molecules will move along the boundaries between aggregated water sub-cells with the resonant frequency that washes through the cell volume. Changes in "stiffness" of within-cell, water sub-cells due to changes in thickness of aggregated water or overall hydration level of the cell will affect vibration induced movement, too. It's a system.

### CindyB — 2015-01-23T03:36:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-9-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12199/post-153650>
