### Josh — 2014-12-01T00:12:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-10-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12245/post-148873>

[http://www.magnetage.com/The\_Figure\_Eight\_WYWS.html](http://www.magnetage.com/The_Figure_Eight_WYWS.html)

> **
> The Figure Eight**
>
>
>
> No other scientists in history have made more fundamental discoveries about magnetism than Albert Roy Davis and Walter C. Rawls, Jr. Many years ago Davis discovered that magnetic energies _spin,_ North pole in a counterclockwise rotation, and the South pole in a clockwiserotation_._ It is now common to read about the spin or vortex of magnetic energies in scientific literature, but what is never mentioned in these publications is the fact that this was first discovered by Davis. Even scientists and researchers of unorthodox science often fail to give credit to Davis and Rawls for their discoveries, yet many of _them_ are well aware of their work.
>
> Davis also discovered that magnetism _flows_ in and around a magnet in the form of a figure "8". The method developed by Michael Faraday in 1852 to show the "lines of force" (Davis discovered that they are actually _cables of force_) around a magnet is**incorrect**. Faraday used a flat piece of paper, placed iron filings on it, and brought a magnet up under the paper to show the lines of force. The truth is that when each particle of steel or iron filing is placed in the field of the magnet, it becomes temporarily magnetized, in effect, becoming a magnet itself. As each particle then attracts and repels, the demonstration presents a mistaken concept.
>
> Albert Roy Davis discovered the figure "8" after developing a special type of electron photography to photograph the magnetic field of a magnet, the same method that was used to take the photo on the right. If you examine this photo you can clearly see the spin of the cables of force.
>
> Davis and Rawls also devised a more simplified demonstration to show the lines of force (i.e. _cables of force_), an experiment that can be performed in any classroom (see photos below). The method requires a large, clear glass container filled with water, some barium ferrite, and a long cylinder or bar magnet attached to a non-magnetic handle. The barium ferrite is put into the water, and the magnet with the handle is used to briskly stir the water to suspend the particles. Next, the stirring ceases and the magnet is held vertically in the center of the container. The particles will then form and reveal the figure eight of magnetic energies. This demonstration should be in all of the science textbooks that are used to educate students about magnetism... but it is not.
>
>
>
> **1\. Place magnet in container**
>
>
>
> **2\. Stir the water so the barium ferrite is suspended**
>
>
>
> **3\. Observe the figure eight of magnetic energies**

### Josh — 2014-12-01T00:16:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-10-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12245/post-148874>

[http://www.thunderbolts.info/forum/phpBB3/viewtopic.php?p=19579](http://www.thunderbolts.info/forum/phpBB3/viewtopic.php?p=19579)

> Albert Roy Davis was the first scientist in the world to discover that the two poles of magnetism, North and South, are separate and distinct energies. North pole magnetism has the opposite effect on matter as South pole magnetism. Davis made numerous discoveries that other scientists and/or doctors now claim to be their own discoveries.
>
> As I've already mentioned, it was Davis that first discovered that the North and South poles are separate energies. Another discovery of his is that the "lines of force" of magnetism do not flow around a magnet the way it is presented in textbooks to this day. The magnetic field leaves one pole, travels in a semicircle around to the exact center of the magnet (the Bloch Wall), reverses polarity, and then travels in a semicircle to the opposite pole. The magnetic fields are flowing in both directions simultaneously. Davis also discovered that magnetic energy does not have "lines of force", it has "cables of force", and he photographed these cables of force in the first picture ever taken of magnetic energy. Here is a quote from Magnetism and Its Effects on the Living System, his first book. "The spin effect is also noticeable by a pull, an electronic vortex twist, that appears on the screen at the outside edge of each cable and/or the roster." [http://keelynet.com/energy/rawls1.htm](http://keelynet.com/energy/rawls1.htm)
>
> Davis was the first to discover that there are two vortices of magnetic energy at each pole, not one. Here is another quote from Magnetism and Its Effects on the Living System. "Within the wide diameter vortex of a magnet's pole is a similar attracting vortex spin. The magnet's spinning tornado-like vortex contains two expressions of power and energy. The electron spin acts as a force of spin direction, and the inner or reverse vortex located within the center of the electron fields acts to form an uptake of energy or a downward thrust of energy. The reversal of the spin effect results in the reversal of the inner vortex reaction." This book, the first by Davis and Walter C. Rawls, Jr., was first published in 1974, however Davis had informed many other scientists and doctors from several foreign nations of his discoveries. He had been in contact with these scientists and doctors for decades prior to the publication of the book. [http://lifeforcegenie.com/problems-with-magnetics.html](http://lifeforcegenie.com/problems-with-magnetics.html)
>
> Davis discovered that at the center of a magnet, at the Bloch Wall, the energies of a magnet change polarities in a broken figure eight. Once again, this is taken from Magnetism and Its Effects... "In the study of the unification of opposing potentials, we find on examining the energy there is the presence of the figure "8". We see this by making a visual study of the effects of the magnet's energies on the screen of a large cathode ray oscilloscope." "The upper part of this broken 8 shows a negative magnetic force, while the bottom is shown to have a positive charge and also reverses the direction of the spin."
>
> Albert Roy Davis was the first to discover that each pole has a different effect on biological systems. He found in his research that the North pole energy has great healing potential, and the South pole can be very dangerous. The North pole energy eliminated or greatly improved many health problems. The South pole energy, for the most part, worsened health problems. Do magnetic fields from powerlines cause cancer? South pole magnetic fields can cause many health problems, and cancer is one of them according to Davis.[http://www.pubmedcentral.nih.gov/articl ... id=2626995](http://www.pubmedcentral.nih.gov/articlerender.fcgi?artid=2626995)
>
> Scientists have an incorrect understanding of the true nature of magnetism, so the anwers they are searching for will not be forthcoming. Here is another brief explanation by Davis and Rawls in their fourth book, The Magnetic Blueprint of Life. "Gravity, electricity, the weak force, the strong force, and such, cannot be scientifically examined for more understanding of Nature and the universe without the acceptance and adoption of the separate natural energy of magnetism. Subatomic physics--the proton, electron, neutron, smaller atomic particles--is incapable of furthering studies for clearer understanding without natural magnetism's separate energies." "Magnetism has been relegated to an inferior position in relation to electricity and gravity. As long as this concept exists in the scientific community, there will be only theories on theories, confusion on confusion."
>
> There is plenty more that I could add to this, but I think I've made my point. Davis and Rawls were geniuses and I think we should give them the credit they deserve. If you feel so inclined to learn more about Davis and Rawls, you'll find that I've only scratched the surface of their incredible discoveries. Take a look at this.[http://www.teslatech.info/ttstore/repor ... /scope.htm](http://www.teslatech.info/ttstore/report/articles/v3n1art/scope.htm)

The last place I might expect to "get" plasma would be in a Charles Missler talk because I had never heard of him and am not drawn to his religious views. Still, it was great listening to him and I suddenly saw a picture of "Plasma" that demystified it for me. As I understand it, "Plasma" is the the energetic line where enough energy is added to a system to liberate the atoms and subatomic particles so that their individual charges and motions come into play independent of the molecules that they were part of in lower energy/entropy states. This clearly opens the door for the "4th Phase" stuff and points towards novel reintegrations/transformations of particles and forces independent of their lower entropy molecular origins with corresponding phase diagram states and interactions. Missler did a really good job of pointing out how poorly oriented most people are to scale/magnitude with good examples one might encounter each day. Overall, the world looks more "fluid" and filled with "possibilities" today.....

### Josh — 2014-12-01T05:01:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-10-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12245/post-148904>

> Food for the Moon (And Other Charged Cometary Bodies)
>
> Before I conclude, I want to mention some classic text from P.D. Ouspensky's book _In Search of the Miraculous_ where he quotes the mystic and spiritual teacher George Ivanovich Gurdjieff in a talk given in Moscow almost a century ago. Gurdjieff, as some know, was a man who possessed a great deal of knowledge in esoteric matters, although it is unclear exactly where he received his knowledge. Those who have taken the time to study Gurdjieff know that he was dead-on with his descriptions of psychology and psychological development. Modern psychology and neuroscience are just starting to catch up with him in many ways.
>
> [](http://www.sott.net/image/s2/54537/full/gurdjieff.jpg)
> © Unknown
> George Ivanovich Gurdjieff
> Gurdjieff also made a lot of interesting remarks about the sciences and man's place in the Universe. One of his most cryptic remarks revolves around Man's relationship to the Moon. He stated flat-out that we are "food for the Moon". Many believe this phrase holds some deep esoteric meaning -- and it very well might -- but there might be a 'plain sight' scientific basis for it as well. Gurdjieff also made some other interesting comments about the Moon which fit closely with what McCanney says. In talking about the possible energy exchanges between the Earth and the Moon, Gurdjieff states:This energy is collected and preserved in a huge accumulator situated on the earth's surface. This accumulator is organic life on earth. Organic life on earth feeds the moon. Everything living on the earth, people, animals, plants, is food for the moon. The moon is a huge living being feeding upon all that lives and grows on the earth. **The moon could not exist without organic life on earth, any more than organic life on earth could exist without the moon. Moreover, in relation to organic life, the moon is a huge electromagnet. If the action of the electromagnet were suddenly to stop, organic life would crumble to nothing.**What he says here makes sense if we consider that the Moon may be necessary for sustaining the Earth's broad magnetic field which protects Man and all other creatures from cosmic radiation. How could organic life on the surface of the Earth survive without such an ideal magnetic environment? It couldn't -- at least not on the surface. And if what McCanney theorizes is true, then the Moon is an indispensable part of the Earth's unique magnetic environment. Also, Gurdjieff mentions that the moon is a "huge electromagnet" in relation to life on Earth. Keep in mind that Gurdjieff made this statement in the very early 1900's before there was any knowledge of electric effects in the solar system and the planets. Gurdjieff was certainly ahead of his time it seems.

[http://www.sott.net/article/221977-Pole-Shift-Look-to-the-Skies](http://www.sott.net/article/221977-Pole-Shift-Look-to-the-Skies)

> **IDEA BOMB:** When light is the moving, just changing of the electro-magnetic field in space won’t bend light at all. ### Da-mo — 2014-12-01T15:37:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-10-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12245/post-148932>

From T10 blog

> Here is where I depart from modern physics. They believe gravity alone can do this. I don’t. That force is not strong enough at these scales. It might be in a star but not in a mitochondria. I do believe the electromagnetic and gravitational force come together, to act in unison in a cell, and are strong enough to cause this interaction to begin. I think the electric attraction is the more fundamental force at play in this interaction.

> When protons fuse in the sun they release massive amounts of electromagnetic radiation from their kinetic energy stores that we call sunlight. For this process to occur, two hydrogen nuclei have to be able to get very close in order to fuse to overcome a Coulomb repulsive force. The closer they get the more they become an ionic [plasma](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plasma_\(physics\)).[Plasma](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plasma_\(physics\)) is the most abundant form of matter in the universe by far. The closer protons get, the stronger the repulsive force between them becomes.

Since gravity is inversely proportional to distance - would that not mean that as things got closer together, gravity would increase in magnitude to partially offset the repulsive force?

Additionally, what if we thought of a concentric spherical multistage compression of EMF in order to overcome the repulsive force? Here I am thinking of an EMF sphere inside an EMF sphere inside an EMF sphere . . . . we could have a potential across the surface of each sphere which would add together as we moved toward the center - sharing the total potential difference required across multiple fields or membranes thus creating a substantial implosive force from the outside in. A multistage spherical transformer of sorts that transforms low EMF on the outer layers to high EMF in the center.

We already know that the potential per unit distance across mitochondrial membranes is substantial and that the protons are being moved to the interior. If double layers and multiple double layers exist as we move outwards from a source, why not consider that they can also be formed inwards in fractal fashion? Would this not fit with the idea of controlling entropy as each sphere sets the background environment of the next sphere inside it?

Also, being a volumetric geometrical progression, the field density required would be inversely proportional to the cube of the distance from the center. The field stress of each sphere would be a product of the potential difference through its thickness and the cubed distance from the center.

What I'm trying to describe is a multi-layered and electromagnetically determined and controlled implosion.

Could this be happening inside the sun? and since the photosphere is a lower temperature than the corona - is it possible that the interior is at still lower temperatures leading to cold fusion?

Too bad the funding was cut on spheromaks for fusion. They seem to have some parallels with mitochondrial membranes since electric current flows through their shells

[http://plasma.physics.swarthmore.edu/ssx/faq.html](http://plasma.physics.swarthmore.edu/ssx/faq.html)

### Josh — 2014-12-01T17:56:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-10-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12245/post-148940>

[http://www.newyorker.com/magazine/2014/03/03/a-star-in-a-bottle](http://www.newyorker.com/magazine/2014/03/03/a-star-in-a-bottle)

> [](http://www.newyorker.com/wp-content/uploads/2014/03/140303_r24681-862.jpg)
> Commercial reactors modelled on ITER could generate power with no carbon, virtually no pollution, and scant radioactive waste.CREDITILLUSTRATION BY JACOB ESCOBEDO
> Years from now—maybe in a decade, maybe sooner—if all goes according to plan, the most complex machine ever built will be switched on in an Alpine forest in the South of France. The machine, called the International Thermonuclear Experimental Reactor, or ITER, will stand a hundred feet tall, and it will weigh twenty-three thousand tons—more than twice the weight of the Eiffel Tower. At its core, densely packed high-precision equipment will encase a cavernous vacuum chamber, in which a super-hot cloud of heavy hydrogen will rotate faster than the speed of sound, twisting like a strand of DNA as it circulates. The cloud will be scorched by electric current (a surge so forceful that it will make lightning seem like a tiny arc of static electricity), and bombarded by concentrated waves of radiation. Beams of uncharged particles—the energy in them so great it could vaporize a car in seconds—will pour into the chamber, adding tremendous heat. In this way, the circulating hydrogen will become ionized, and achieve temperatures exceeding two hundred million degrees Celsius—more than ten times as hot as the sun at its blazing core.

This theory also proposes gravity as an emergent property of pressure gradient across a vortex - redefining an absolute vacuum and not just an absence of matter, but "a volume with no space in it" since space is not empty.

He makes a very interesting observation about the human ear - why is a single organ used for the dual purpose of sensing both pressure variations and orientation relative to gravity? Does one callibrate the other so that pressure imbalances in pressure do not cause errors in orientation? Are pressure and gravity fundamentally linked?

Could then this scale down to where the gravity inside an atom in induced by a balance between subatomic particle orbits and an induced absolute vacuum on the centre of the nucleus?

It could also add to the the observation that sunspots are black and might be holes in the shell revealing an absolute vacuum inside - possibly dark because the absence of matter means there is nothing for light to fall incident upon. > The electron, shown in green, takes its place beside the centre down-quark and the up-quarks orbit both. The electron is drawn the same size as the other particles for the reasons given in the [particle physics](http://forum.jackkruse.com/Particles.htm#bold-hypothesis) chapter. In this way it can overlap the up-quarks, thus making it stable. This would not be possible if electrons were much smaller as might be expected based on their mass. As with the proton, the particles are slightly overlapping. The neutron is shown face-on so all its particles are visible. Now here are 3D versions of the proton and neutron. The particles are drawn with “wireframes” so their rotation can be properly seen.

and is this graphic showing the beginning of beta decay?

[http://www.alternativephysics.org/book/NucleonStructure.htm](http://www.alternativephysics.org/book/NucleonStructure.htm)

> **Neutron instability**
> Stable? Okay so a neutron isn’t completely stable. It’s well acknowledged that isolated neutrons will decay into a proton and electron – the neutron having a half-life of 10.3 minutes. If we look again at the neutron structure, both the down-quark and the electron occupy the same position within the structure. But the electron is extremely light. It is 206 and 815 times lighter than the down and up quarks respectively. With two heavy up-quarks in orbit around it, one might expect that the electron would have a hard time sitting still. Perhaps it will rattle about. Here’s an animation showing a possible electron wobble during the neutron rotation (again with wireframes, and two small dots to help see the wobble):
>
> As the up-quarks spin in one direction, the electron perhaps bounces between them and perhaps rotates a bit. However this can’t continue indefinitely. This movement creates an instability in the electron which could cause it to become unstuck. **On the other hand if the neutron was attached to a proton, such as within an atom’s nucleus, the electron would be held steady and there would be no instability.**

Hold on a minute!!! Hydrogen has only one proton and if that gets used up creating a neutron then there is no other proton available to stabilise that neutron - and hence the neutron is unstable and more reversable back to a proton. Rogue element indeed!!!

Looking at the neutron above compared to the proton . . . does the neutron have a gap in the middle whereas the proton doesn't? Is there more gravity in the hydrogen atom turned neutron than there is in the hydrogen atom?

Also, at the 8:40 mark of the hollow sun video above showing the spheromak like graphic - this would explain how the north and south poles are created as the result of current flowing through a toroid.

### Da-mo — 2014-12-02T07:00:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-10-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12245/post-148997>

Finally got round to watching the rest of the December webinar. A few thoughts . . .

**Tilt angle and seasonal time** . . . From the tilt papers linked above

> What that means is that any perturbation, even one over a limited time, must create a tilt. I agree that the Earth feels a perturbation from Jupiter only about once a year, but it feels it every year, year in year out. It also feels it over an extended period, even if that period is only a few weeks. It isn't hit by a few photons in a passing wave, it is hit by an extended force. The Earth cannot just shrug off this force. If we add to that the perturbations from the other Jovians, we find that the Earth is almost always in the rebound stage from a perturbation from one of the four. Remember, Uranus' perturbation is larger than Jupiter's, and Neptune's is more than twice as big. Because the response to these perturbations is slow, and the rebound from each perturbation is slow, there is time for them to add. [http://milesmathis.com/tilt2.html](http://milesmathis.com/tilt2.html)

It would seem that there are even longer "seasonal" fluctuations than the annual ones as the earth tilt is affected by the other planets.

**Vibration and oscillation** . . . . this would explain why it is easier to stay awake when moving around (adding oscillation to the system) - say if working nightshift or very extended hours - and why taking a break and being relatively still causes a wave (pun intended) of tiredness to sweep over me (removing oscillation and leaving only vibration). Also why you can wake someone by shaking them.

> **Always alert**
> Our sense of hearing is the one sense that never sleeps. It evolved to protect us from ambush at night. At the Sleep Laboratory in Swansea, Dr Mark Blagrove plays sounds to sleeping volunteers. He finds that, although they can shut out sounds when asleep, their sense of hearing is constantly alert. [http://www.bbc.co.uk/science/humanbody/tv/humansenses/programme5.shtml](http://www.bbc.co.uk/science/humanbody/tv/humansenses/programme5.shtml)

Might there be other reasons that our hearing remains intact during sleep that relate to the quantum effects of a vibration only environment enabling entanglement? Do the balance organs in the ear need to continue to function so that we can sense orientation relative to gravity while we sleep?

Really curious about the effect of music now from this perspective - if light can heal or destroy coherence, what effects can sound have?

**Water is a quasi-crystal** . . . that caught my attention after recently watching a video on harnessing environmental EMF using quasi-crystals.

So . . . . if water is a quasi-crystal, it is an antenna absorbing all EMF radiation both native and non native and these signals are getting mixed in with the communications between cellular and mitochondrial DNA.

So . . . if water is an antenna and Faraday cages can be used as antennae to absorb EMF and direct the signals to ground to protect us from nnEMF . . . . is quasi-crystal water doing the same thing when we ground ourselves?

### Da-mo — 2014-12-02T07:26:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-10-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12245/post-148999>

> [Da-mo said:](/goto/post?id=148986)
>
> If the above is held correct, then gravity is not proportional to mass - it is proportional to the absence of mass at the center of a vortex. So it would seem gravity is an emergent property of mass in motion around an absolute vacuum. The pressure differential between them causes them to be attracted.

Hmmm . . . not so sure about this idea now - if it were true we should be able to create gravity with a centrifuge and an electric field. Is that what they are trying to do at CERN? [http://home.web.cern.ch/about/physics/extra-dimensions-gravitons-and-tiny-black-holes](http://home.web.cern.ch/about/physics/extra-dimensions-gravitons-and-tiny-black-holes)

It seems to me they could ask this question in a better way. They are not looking for an absolute vacuum - they are looking for a black hole. WIki describes a black hole thus

> A black hole is a region of spacetime from which gravity prevents anything, including light, from escaping. **The theory of general relativity predicts that a sufficiently compact mass will deform spacetime to form a black hole.** The boundary of the region from which no escape is possible is called the event horizon. [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Black\_hole](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Black_hole)

Yet the hollow sun theory (HST) would have that gravity is partly emergent from an absence of mass - an absolute vacuum (with mass moving around it - and not so sure it even has to be moving - maybe it needs to be moving in order to maintain the absolute vacuum while still allowing gravitational permeability). Also, while CW might define as a distortion in space-time, the HST as described in the earlier video redefined an absolute vacuum as an absence of space.

Vacuum or gravity or vacuum-gravity?

Kind of like they have the problem backwards with cause and effect switched around. I dunno, smarter people than me working there.

Just watched The Primer Feilds series. It tied a lot of things together for me. Magnetism, wave-particle duality, the bending and slowing of light and how matter is energy condensed. Im also starting to get a visual picture of entanglement although still a bit foggy. I was still very disappointedto find the last 4 parts are not available yet.

If this theory is correct, then using electromagnetic radiation to observe the universe seems like looking at it in a hall of distorted mirrors - magnetic feilds and mediums over these vast distances would distort what we observe in terms of direction bending of light), wavelength (compression of light) and intensity (diffusion and diffraction of light).

It does however open up several possibilities biology might use for manipulation of light.

I also note that credit does note seem to be given to the scientists that discovered the underlying basis of this theory.

Rawls, Jr. Many years ago Davis discovered that magnetic energies _spin,_ North pole in a counterclockwise rotation, and the South pole in a clockwiserotation_._ It is now common to read about the spin or vortex of magnetic energies in scientific literature, but what is never mentioned in these publications is the fact that this was first discovered by Davis. ### Da-mo — 2014-12-04T00:38:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-10-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12245/post-149136>

In relation to December webinar regarding vibration and oscillation . . .

My physio has a new toy called a "Gravity revolution". [http://www.gravityhq.co.nz/NZ/index.html](http://www.gravityhq.co.nz/NZ/index.html) It is a vibration training and therapy machine. You can vary both the magnitude and frequency of the angular oscillation of the platform. Its similar to a Vibra-Train just a different brand.

I had a 15 minute session on it yesterday and noticed some interesting effects.

-   At 5Hz it felt as if it induced a tranverse wave from left to right in my body propogating from feet to head.
-   At 8Hz it felt as if the wave rotated 90 degrees, still propogating from feet to head, but the wave plane was now front to back.
-   At 12Hz it felt like standing waves and interference patterns were set up in my quadraceps or lower back and I could move the place of maximum vibration but adjusting the tension in my body, or my posture or by chanding my mass distribution by moving my arms up or down.
-   At 15Hz the most interest sensation happened - I can only describe it as a spiral, possibly 2 counter=rotating spirals propogating from feet to head.
-   After 15 minutes playing with this machine which is purely mechanical in nature, my feet were tingling and I felt myself slip into a somewhat meditative state.

If the spiral sensation I was sensing at 15Hz has any significance, it makes me wonder if a z pinch could be induced and what effect that would have - condensing of matter?

I'm also contemplating my experience with the vibration machine in terms of this . . .

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=148825)
>
> We have two forces at 90 degrees........one is tied to potential energy and the other to kinetic.

We know that light (EMF) exerts a force on any surface it is reflected by or enters or leaves (as in translucent matter) and we know that biological materials can be translucent/reflective/magnetic etc and also piezo-electric. We know waveforms can structure our matter. Given the piezo-electric effect, it is concievable that vibrational waves and oscillations can be used to not only structure our matter, but charge or discharge it electrically as well.

Can the application of kinetic vibration have beneficial effects electromagnetically? - maybe this is another way to apply Shumann resonance to a system which can link EMF and kinetic energy via tensegrity structures.

Order from chaos

Sidenote: It was very interesting watching the reaction of my Labradors to the sound frequencies in the video

### Jack Kruse — 2014-12-04T05:23:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-10-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12245/post-149188>

And here is the key study just out: [http://www.cell.com/cell-metabolism/abstract/S1550-4131(14)00498-7](http://www.cell.com/cell-metabolism/abstract/S1550-4131\(14\)00498-7)

You know better than most how I really feel........too many people think its food........when its just not. Trees and plants only need water sun and CO2 and they have 100% circadian followship............There is a deep lesson here. Water, CO2 vary.........but light and day is about constant as the environment gets. We just fail to account for it in all our experiments.

When we introduce circadian mismatches energy flows slows down in us, its action force masses to becomes tangible matter, the substances we think we know best because we can observe it and sense it. The problem is we are fooled by what we think we now know, because we have no idea what we are missing in nature’s recipes. What we see is not the truth, it is an opinion of the truth. What happens during day time and night time is radically different and hides nature's recipes. Science is ever changing, and incomplete, but it always maintains its own resistance to new and better ideas.
Metaphors reign large in modern biochemistry, where quantum mysteries lurk just below our perception. It is all electrons and protons no matter how much you dont want to believe it.

### Da-mo — 2014-12-04T14:44:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-10-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12245/post-149209>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=149146)
>

Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }
>
>
>
> So if you look at that picture.........how would the wave move consider the electric displacement and the magnetic flux? Think about a wave in water or in the wind with this picture interacting.................every movement in the universe is the result of this one picture.

Not sure if the question referred to my experience with the machine at the physio - but I'm going to have a go applying it anyway. . . .

Vibration is a longitudinal wave - meaning the displacement is parallel to the direction of propagation - so I will apply it along the axis of the electromagnetic waveform.

**Rigid/Displaceable vertical axis - Oscillation**
If the vertical axis had a vibration applied to the end of it in the direction same direction as the vertical axis it would oscillate up and down - if the vertical axis were rigid. Here we would be dealing with an oscillation . . . . I'll deal with a vibrational wave first.

**Compliant/Compressible vertical axis (non displaceable, fixed in space) - Vibration**

If the vertical axis were compliant, it would be alternately compressed and stretched by the vibration. As the vertical axis was compressed it would be analogous to the wave coming ashore in the seashore diagram above. The reducing depth of the rising shoreline imitates the building pressure of a vibrational wave.

1\. Full cycle compression: vibrational wavelength = 2 x electromagnetic wavelength, in phase

-   This would cause the magnetic force and electric displacement to increase in magnitude and decrease in wavelength - just as a wave coming ashore. As the vertical axis was stretched, the converse would happen.

2\. Half cycle compression/distension: vibrational wavelength = electromagnetic wavelength, in phase

-   If the wavelength of the vibration matched the electromagnetic wavelength and was in phase with the electric and magnetic waveforms - it would stretch and flatten one half cycle while shortening and steepening the other half cycle. The total wavelength would remain the same. the magnitudes of one half cycle would be exchanged for displacement along the vertical axis of the other half cycle. One half cycle shorter in duration and larger in amplitude, the other half cycle longer in duration and smaller in magnitude.

**So what would this mean if we applied these vibrationally modified waveforms to a semiconductor?**

If we superimpose a semiconductor onto the diagram immediately above by making the vertical axis a thicker line - the thickness of the line would represent the voltage below which no current flows - the threshold voltage. Only to the extent that the waveform protrudes above the line will electrical current flow.

-   In example 1. to the extent that the electromagnetic magnitudes exceed the semiconductor threshold voltage, current will flow in **both** directions in pulsatile fashion.

-   In example 2. to the extent that the electromagnetic magnitudes exceed the semiconductor threshold voltage, current will flow in **one** direction in pulsatile fashion.

Does example 1. comprise a transistor or amplifier effect?
Does example 2. comprise a rectifier effect?

Can vibration be used to amplify and rectify electrical flow in biological semiconductors?

**What happens if the waveforms are out of phase?**

Destructive interference?
Secondary and tertiary waves?

**Currently pondering the implications of the above in peizo-electric collagen fibres with its triple helix structure and other fibres and filaments with their double counter-wound helixes. . . .**

Are the three helixes in collagen all wound the same way like a piece of rope or are they counter-wound inside each other?

Do counter wound helixes allow current to flow one way through one helix and the other way through another helix? - The contraction and extension of muscle fibres?

Can currents flow in opposite direction simultaneously through the layers of the same fibre the way Becker's DC current flows between the axon and the myelin sheath?

Is collagen like a wound guitar string . . . tunable to a frequency by tension?

What is the plural of helix? helixes? heli? - answered, its helices

I note that when I felt the spiralling sensation on the vibrating/oscillating machine it was set at 15Hz - about 2 x the Schumann resonance. Also remembering afterwards slipping into a meditative state and thinking about how Schumann resonance frequency = alpha brain wave frequency.

### av8r — 2014-12-04T18:32:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-10-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12245/post-149231>

"Linearly polarized light is converted to circularly polarized light by slowing one component of the field. Effectively, the light is given a twist. This is achieved by using a material that transmits light at different speeds depending on its polarity. This happens because of the way the atoms are arranged in the crystal. In the diagram above, you can see a linearly polarized light wave (simple wave entering at left) oriented at 45 degrees to the system. Projected onto the vertical and horizontal planes are the vertical and horizontal components of that wave (equal and in phase). When that wave passes through the material, the vertical component is slowed down so that it is out of phase with the horizontal component. When it is a quarter wavelength slower, the resulting wave is helical. To an observer receiving the circularly polarized light, the electric field of that light will appear to rotate rather than just bob up and down. Hence the term circular polarization"
[http://kightleys.photoshelter.com/gallery-image/Physics/G0000Oh2DVMhYr.0/I0000XkPmUFv5qiw](http://kightleys.photoshelter.com/gallery-image/Physics/G0000Oh2DVMhYr.0/I0000XkPmUFv5qiw)

While we are on the subject about waves, the December Webinar brought up the idea of waking hours consisting of both oscillation and vibration and the sleeping hours of vibration only when we are incapable of movement. So it would seem that oscillation is tied to movement. This bring to mind a number of points to consider.

What then is vibration tied to?

Are both oscillation and vibration tied to the circadian rhythm of the planet in terms of magnetism and Schumann resonance being night/day cycles magnitude-wise?

Thinking of a reason for the necessity during sleep of having a vibration only mode I remember a Dr. Bonlie (sp?) Q&A where he was saying that the brain communicates with the various tissues during sleep at separate distinct frequencies - both receiving information and issuing instructions.

Is the necessity of having no oscillation present during this communication to do with reducing "static" or "white noise" from the information exchange?

Does sleeping in an EMF polluted environment obfuscate these communications?

Waves can travel through oscillations, but they may be slightly phase shifted by them and this would affect resonance. So it would seem that a resonance is an important part of this communication process.

I wonder if this is why the ear doesn't sleep - is it necessary for calibrating or tuning out background resonances and vibrations like a filter for internal resonances?

Does the ear contain the organ of balance and orientation to the earth so that the rotation or angle of the vibrations and oscillations it senses can be accounted and adjusted for?

Does deafness or wearing earplugs during sleep have health implications in the same way that being blind does in the ability to sense the environment, yoke to the environment and receive information and energy?

Just more pieces of the puzzle for me to wonder where they fit and if I'm looking at them the right way up.

This is something that I've been wondering about for the last few months…I started to wear ear plugs two years ago, they seem to help turn off the super alert, high cortisol state that I found myself in at night. I have been so happy with my sleep during this time….but now I realize I've got some tinnitus that I only hear when things are quiet…could this be the ear increasing it's sensitivity to compensate for the dampening of sound??? It's only in my right ear (along with TMJ, bone degeneration etc) and it is most often the notes of middle A and G switching back and forth rapidly. Every now and then it changes pitch and I'll go figure out the new note on the piano. It is not loud. I've always been very prone to ear worms and music is an almost a constant inner companion. When it get's to loud, I limit my noise environment and spend more time outside.

Cortisol is lower these days, so I may try to sleep without this crutch….I'm sure some of this goes back to the hydration issue that still seems to lie at the bottom of all my conditions…oscillations and vibrations in mud instead of water so to speak.

### Da-mo — 2014-12-05T15:51:34+0200

Yesterday in between nightshifts I slept without earplugs for the first time in ages. I usually wear them if I sleep during the day so that the rest of the household can go about their day without waking me. With earplugs I find I sleep maybe 4 or 5 hours. Today I slept 9. It may also be due to wearing blue blockers for the whole previous nightshift rather than just the last few hours before sleep. I also have tinnitus and the last time I can remember not having it was during a short 2 week period while moving house where:

-   I had no time for internet or TV (and they were in transit anyway)
-   I was not smoking
-   I was getting lots of exercise moving house and preparing the old place for sale
-   I was on leave so was not exposed to the EMF at work
-   I was not doing shift work due to being on leave
-   Because I was not doing shift work and sleeping at normal times, I did not wear earplugs.

### Da-mo — 2014-12-05T16:00:09+0200

Your post prompted me to like at some pictures of the helical structures and notice that rather than a continuous spiral that they are made of shorter lengths connected together in tensegrity fashion. Now I'm wondering if these are "tuned" lengths that have a specific resonance important to how they capture and distribute waves.

An interesting thing about magnetically coupled (like some tensegrity structures) electrical equipment (inductive and capacitive circuits/motors/transformers) is that a phase shift occurs between the current and voltage both across the coupling and in relation to them individually (current and voltage) in the same way that capacitors cause phase shifts. I'm sure it all fits together somehow.

I have many more questions than answers, but every so often something clicks and the dominoes fall, so I'll keep asking them.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-12-06T00:25:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-10-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12245/post-149327>

> [Da-mo said:](/goto/post?id=149311)
>
> Your post prompted me to like at some pictures of the helical structures and notice that rather than a continuous spiral that they are made of shorter lengths connected together in tensegrity fashion. Now I'm wondering if these are "tuned" lengths that have a specific resonance important to how they capture and distribute waves.
>
> An interesting thing about magnetically coupled (like some tensegrity structures) electrical equipment (inductive and capacitive circuits/motors/transformers) is that a phase shift occurs between the current and voltage both across the coupling and in relation to them individually (current and voltage) in the same way that capacitors cause phase shifts. I'm sure it all fits together somehow.
>
> I have many more questions than answers, but every so often something clicks and the dominoes fall, so I'll keep asking them.

Paper '**Structure and Organization of Actin Filaments**' -- [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK9908/](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK9908/)

Each Actin filament is definitely a helix

(interesting picture showing threadmilling)

As noted earlier, the two ends of an [actin](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A2889/) filament grow at different rates, with monomers being added to the fast-growing end (the plus end) five to ten times faster than to the slow-growing (minus) end.

Because ATP-actin dissociates less readily than ADP-actin, this results in a difference in the critical concentration of monomers needed for polymerization at the two ends. This difference can result in the phenomenon known as **treadmilling**, which illustrates the dynamic behavior of actin filaments ([Figure 11.4](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK9908/figure/A1769/?report=objectonly)).

For the system to be at an overall steady state, **the concentration of free actin monomers must be intermediate between the critical concentrations required for polymerization at the plus and minus ends of the actin filaments**.

Under these conditions, there is a net loss of monomers from the minus end, which is balanced by a net addition to the plus end.

Treadmilling requires ATP, with ATP-actin polymerizing at the plus end of filaments while ADP-actin dissociates from the minus end. Although the role of treadmilling in the cell is unclear, it may reflect the dynamic assembly and disassembly of actin filaments required for cells to move and change shape.​

Below is an image of actin filaments cross-linked by fimbrin. Guess what, the distance between parallel filaments is always 14nm (140 angstroms). Different cross linking proteins will put a different distance between the actin filaments, but it's always a fixed distance. Your theory about tuning sounds to be right

Final image showing Integrins (which are transmembrane proteins) binding to uniformly distributed bundles of actin filaments (known as stress fibers)

Stress fibers are contractile bundles of [actin](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A2889/) filaments, crosslinked by α-actinin, that anchor the cell and exert tension against the substratum. They are attached to the [plasma membrane](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3256/) at focal adhesions via interactions with [integrin](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3132/).

(note: a-actinin cross-linked actin form actin filaments separated by 40nm)​

...

### yewwei.tan — 2014-12-06T00:52:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-10-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12245/post-149328>

Right now, I'm modelling the actin and integrin network as a means to transform and transfer energy. The side effect of that is being able to sense changes in energy levels in various parts of the system, and mount the appropriate response.

That's not too unlike at least some of the job of @[Da-mo](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/9712/), which is to be the smarts behind power allocation based upon built-in inspection and feedback mechanisms in the power grid.

But I've been assuming that the actin and integrin network is nothing but a power grid. Sure, it is a big store of water, but the H+ of that water can't be used for energy generation unless you have the conditions described in Tensegrity 10 and 11 (which are only present in mitochondria).

I've been starting to think that this network is just really efficient, and any energy transfer lost in transferring vibrational and oscillatory information ends up as water between the network, which can still be used, albeit at a much slower rate, and requiring more work to get out of.

Of course, there has to be some amount of heat loss, to create the right amount of EZ water to "lubricate" the network. Again, we bring up the question of purposeful destructive wave interference to generate heat at specific junctions of the network.

More questions and no answers ....

### Da-mo — 2014-12-06T02:03:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-10-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12245/post-149333>

Perhaps this will tie it all together . . .

> **F-actin, when it is placed in a field of sonic vibration, catalyzes the dephosphorylation of added ATP into ADP** and inorganic phosphate, but maintains its polymerized form. **The dephosphorylation reaction proceeds in proportion to the protein concentration.** When the KCl concentration is varied, the dephosphorylation rate takes a minimum at 0.06 _M_ KCl. The dephosphorylation rate is greatly decreased when Mg2+ is added. The reciprocal of the dephosphorylation rate seems to depend linearly on the reciprocal of the added ATP concentration, although it depends little on this. In G-actin solutions ATP splitting never takes place.
> **These experimental results are explained on the basis of a reaction mechanism proposed, which consists of three steps: (a) loosening of the rigid F-actin structure caused by the vibration, which unmasks the bound ADP**; (b) substitution of tge unmasked ADP by free ATP; (c) irreversible dephosphorylation of the bound ATP into the ADP masked in the rigid structure of the F-actin. [http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/0006300261909040](http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/0006300261909040)

or this

> **Under ultrasonic vibration the bound Ca of F-actin exchanges with free Ca. The exchange of the F-actin-bound Ca is more rapid than the ATP hydrolysis catalyzed by F-actin under the same conditions.**
> In the presence of ATP the bound Ca of F-actin cannot be removed by polyethylene sulfonate or urea on ultrasonic vibration. It can be removed, however, by EDTA or ethylene glycol bis(_β_\-amino-ethyl ether)-_N_\-_N_′-tetraacetic acid without inhibition of the ATPase activity of F-actin.
> The F-actin-bound Ca can also be removed by mercurials with concomitant inhibition of the F-actin ATPase. A relationship appears between ATPase activity of F-actin and those -SH groups which seem necessary for polymerization.
> **The Ca-free F-actin, prepared by ultrasonic vibration in the presence of EDTA and ATP, maintains its viscosity and its ability to interact with L-myosin both at high and low ionic strength; however, it loses its ability to be depolymerized reversibly.**
> The ATPase activity of actomyosin prepared from Ca-free F-actin can be inhibited by EDTA both in the presence and absence of Mg2+. [http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/0006300262903426](http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/0006300262903426)

or this

> **Sonic vibration induces the nucleation of actin in the absence of magnesium ions and cytochalasins inhibit the elongation of the nuclei.**
> [Maruyama K](http://forum.jackkruse.com/pubmed?term=Maruyama%20K%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=6893836).
> **Abstract**
> Polymerization of actin was completely inhibited by metal chelating agents, such as EDTA or diaminocyclohexanetetraacetic acid, even in the presence of 0.1 M KCl and excess ATP. Addition of 1 mM MgCl2, but not CaCl2, caused full polymerization in the presence of 10 mM diaminocyclohexanetetraacetic acid, suggesting that micromolar concentrations of Mg ions are required for the polymerization of actin. **However, it turned out that sonic vibration induced a very rapid polymerization of actin in the absence of Mg ions.** The sonication-induced polymerization of actin was greatly inhibited by cytochalasins D and B, and partially by beta-actinin. This can be explained by assuming that the fast growing ends of the formed nuclei are blocked by the cytochalasins, and the slowly growing ends with beta-actinin. The elongation of added F-actin fragments was also similarly inhibited by the cytochalasins and beta-actinin in the absence of Mg ions. **It appears that sonic vibration enhances nuclei formation of actin monomers by mechanical agitation in the absence of Mg ions, which stabilize formed nuclei.** [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/6893836](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/6893836)

### Jack Kruse — 2014-12-06T15:58:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-10-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12245/post-149350>

Samarium hexaboride, abbreviated SmB6, is a compound made of the metal samarium and the rare metalloid boron. University of Michigan researchers have confirmed its unusual electrical properties and shown how it could advance the development of next-generation transistors for quantum computers. (Image: Gang Li)
These properties are particularly enticing to scientists because SmB6 is considered a strongly correlated material. Its electrons interact more closely with one another than most solids. This helps its interior maintain electricity-blocking behavior.
This deeper understanding of samarium hexaboride raises the possibility that engineers might one day route the flow of electric current in quantum computers like they do on silicon in conventional electronics, said Lu Li, assistant professor of physics in the College of Literature, Science, and the Arts and a co-author of a paper on the findings published in _Science_ (["Two-dimensional Fermi surfaces in Kondo Insulator SmB6"](http://dx.doi.org/doi:10.1126/science.1250366)).
"Before this, no one had found Dirac electrons in a strongly correlated material," Li said. "We thought strong correlation would hurt them, but now we know it doesn't. While I don't think this material is the answer, now we know that this combination of properties is possible and we can look for other candidates."
The drawback of samarium hexaboride is that the researchers only observed these behaviors at ultracold temperatures.
Quantum computers use particles like atoms or electrons to perform processing and memory tasks. They could offer dramatic increases in computing power due to their ability to carry out scores of calculations at once. Because they could factor numbers much faster than conventional computers, they would greatly improve computer security.
In quantum computers, "qubits" stand in for the 0s and 1s of conventional computers' binary code. While a conventional bit can be either a 0 or a 1, a qubit could be both at the same time—only until you measure it, that is. Measuring a quantum system forces it to pick one state, which eliminates its main advantage.
Dirac electrons, named after the English physicist whose equations describe their behavior, straddle the realms of classical and quantum physics, Li said. Working together with other materials, they could be capable of clumping together into a new kind of qubit that would change the properties of a material in a way that could be measured indirectly, without the qubit sensing it. The qubit could remain in both states.
While these applications are intriguing, the researchers are most enthusiastic about the fundamental science they've uncovered.
"In the science business you have concepts that tell you it should be this or that and when it's two things at once, that's a sign you have something interesting to find," said Jim Allen, an emeritus professor of physics who studied samarium hexaboride for 30 years. "Mysteries are always intriguing to people who do curiosity-driven research."
Allen thought for years that samarium hexaboride must be a flawed insulator that behaved like a metal at low temperatures because of defects and impurities, but he couldn't align that with all of its other properties.
"The prediction several years ago about it being a topological insulator makes a lightbulb go off if you're an old guy like me and you've been living with this stuff your whole life," Allen said.
In 2010, Kai Sun, assistant professor of physics at U-M, led a group that first posited that SmB6 might be a topological insulator. He and Allen were also involved in seminal U-M experiments led by physics professor Cagliyan Kurdak in 2012 that showed indirectly that the hypothesis was correct.
"But the scientific community is always critical," Sun said. "They want very strong evidence. We think this experiment finally provides direct proof of our theory."
Source: _University of Michigan_

Read more: [45-year physics mystery shows a path to quantum transistors](http://www.nanowerk.com/nanotechnology-news/newsid=38361.php#ixzz3L7rjHRUp)

### PaulG — 2014-12-09T03:40:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-10-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12245/post-149567>

Sorry to but in so rudely but I discovered a new term today and thought it important to share..FlexoElectricity

See [Electricity and mechanics of biomembrane systems: Flexoelectricity in living membranes](http://www.researchgate.net/profile/Alexander_Petrov7/publication/6079535_Electricity_and_mechanics_of_biomembrane_systems_flexoelectricity_in_living_membranes/links/09e41510036f8c0bc8000000) by **Alexander G. Petrov** from Biomolecular Layers Department, Institute of Solid State Physics, Bulgarian Academy of Sciences, 72 Tzarigradsko Chaussee, 1784 Sofia, Bulgaria

> Flexoelectricity is a liquid crystal analogue of piezoelectricity. Instead of a translational mechanical degree of freedom of a membrane in the case of piezoelectricity (i.e., area stretching,
> thickness compression, etc.), flexoelectricity involves an orientational degree of freedom, the membrane curvature. First striking impression when looking at an electron micrograph of a cell is that cell membranes very often tend to be strongly curved on a nanometer length scale. Unlike tensile or compressive strains, it is very easy to sustain curvature strains (i.e., torques) in a membrane because of the smallness of its curvature elastic modulus K \[7\]. In many cases this curvature is dynamic \[56\].

> Flexoelectricity \[59\] is a fundamental property of liquid crystals, relating their mechanical and electric degrees of freedom. In a membrane system with just 2 degrees of freedom, we can encounter flexoelectric effects discussed above, direct and converse ones. The well-known fact that the inner mitochondrial membrane is quite smooth in the non-energized state, while in energized mitochondria it is extensively curved \[28–31\], is quite indicative to us of a possible role for the flexoeffect in the energy transformation. **Thus, flexoelectricity could be a missing link in the hypothesis of conformational coupling at the oxidative phosphorylation in mitochondria \[32\]. Charging of the membrane capacitor could then originate from curvature-induced flexopolarization.** As we have seen above, in membranes (living and model), flexoelectricity provides a linear relationship between membrane curvature and membrane polarization, or transmembrane voltage and membrane bending stress. It is thus closely related to mechanosensitivity and mechanotransduction, basic features of living systems \[47\].
>
> Earlier, a model using direct flexoelectric effect for transformation of mechanical into electric energy in the hearing process in stereocilia was proposed \[47\]. Recently, the converse flexoeffect that transforms mechanical into electric energy was employed for description of the electromotility of outer hair cell membrane (Fig. 8) \[60\]. Electromotility plays a central role in the process of mechanoampli-fication, which is vital for the hearing of high frequency sounds \[63,64\].

A good definition [here](http://books.google.com.au/books?id=q14rhaV_o1MC&pg=PA83&lpg=PA83&dq=flexoelectricity+in+biological&source=bl&ots=a_J_P6gaul&sig=thZ7isrMiUp65T6jg_hJZoj_xdY&hl=en&sa=X&ei=A0mGVMf8BYWT8QWbtIHYCQ&ved=0CDwQ6AEwBg#v=onepage&q=flexoelectricity%20in%20biological&f=false)

### Jack Kruse — 2014-12-09T04:39:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-10-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12245/post-149578>

This is why D and L states on lipid membranes is important. The December webinar of 2014 begins to crack this door. Chirality is a property of asymmetry important in biology. This is why water abuts it in a cell. Water is also asymmetric because of hydrogen. Asymmetry meeting asymmetry in a cell allows for the possibility of cancellations of vibrations/oscillations. The role of chirality in membrane-forming lipids is not well studied today but I think when people understand European Robin magneto-abilities that will change. The chirality of phospholipids makes fluid lipid bilayers piezoelectric in cell membranes. Chiral lipids play a huge central role in the functioning of cell membranes as active mechanotransducers because they allow for flexoelectric potential to make signaling bidriectionally. Piezoelectric abilities turn mechanical info into electric signals. Collagen and cell membranes are piezoelectric and flexoelectric. This allows them to deal with bidirectional flow of electricity from light. When we compress/condense different lipids in their chiral positions, there can be an induced a "tilt" of the molecules with respect to the bilayer's normal and produced electric current perpendicular to the tilt plane, with the chiral lipids only. **_This "tilt effect" can be very similar to what happens in seasons on this planet._** The Earth's axis actually determines circadian biology. _The chirality in cell membranes does the same thing for a cell._ It changes as season changes. This "_electric tilt_" acts like the magneto-elastic waves used by butterflies and robins in magnetico-navigation with the incident light angle from the sun. This effect is due to the phase of the aperiodic liquid crystal structure of the bilayers, which under this molecular tilt becomes a ferroelectric magnetic domain phase, where the polarization of light is normal to the tilt plane. This magnetoelectric resonance coupling to light allows for a wide variety of sensory possibilities of cell membranes such as mechanoreception, magnetosensitivity, as well as in-plane proton membrane transport and related phenomena such as ATP synthesis, ion channel depolarization, and other soft molecular machine quantum effects due to kinetic isotope effects.

Obviously there are those like @[Da-mo](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/9712/) who can step up their game in step with the information provided, and make the needed mistakes to learn quickly.

On top of that, I'm a believer that the components of the system that is our body (and nature) are simple. Simple things are hard to find, since you have to rule out the other 999,999 possibilities to get to the one mechanism that's correct. The faster the rate of information gathering, the faster the process of discovery.

While I don't really understand Stephen Wolfram's work, his work with cellular automaton is something that is right up my alleyw, and I'm convinced that very simple rules can give rise to very complex interactions.

But the rules are still simple  ..... and once some of us can understand those simple rules, it actually doesn't take a lot of work to explain it to supposedly "scientifically illiterate" people. ### yewwei.tan — 2014-12-09T12:28:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-10-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12245/post-149596>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=149578)
>
> This is why D and L states on lipid membranes is important. The December webinar of 2014 begins to crack this door. Chirality is a property of asymmetry important in biology. This is why water abuts it in a cell. Water is also asymmetric because of hydrogen. Asymmetry meeting asymmetry in a cell allows for the possibility of cancellations of vibrations/oscillations. The role of chirality in membrane-forming lipids is not well studied today but I think when people understand European Robin magneto-abilities that will change. The chirality of phospholipids makes fluid lipid bilayers piezoelectric in cell membranes. Chiral lipids play a huge central role in the functioning of cell membranes as active mechanotransducers because they allow for flexoelectric potential to make signaling bidriectionally. Piezoelectric abilities turn mechanical info into electric signals. Collagen and cell membranes are piezoelectric and flexoelectric. This allows them to deal with bidirectional flow of electricity from light. When we compress/condense different lipids in their chiral positions, there can be an induced a "tilt" of the molecules with respect to the bilayer's normal and produced electric current perpendicular to the tilt plane, with the chiral lipids only. **_This "tilt effect" can be very similar to what happens in seasons on this planet._** The Earth's axis actually determines circadian biology. _The chirality in cell membranes does the same thing for a cell._ It changes as season changes. This "_electric tilt_" acts like the magneto-elastic waves used by butterflies and robins in magnetico-navigation with the incident light angle from the sun. This effect is due to the phase of the aperiodic liquid crystal structure of the bilayers, which under this molecular tilt becomes a ferroelectric magnetic domain phase, where the polarization of light is normal to the tilt plane. This magnetoelectric resonance coupling to light allows for a wide variety of sensory possibilities of cell membranes such as mechanoreception, magnetosensitivity, as well as in-plane proton membrane transport and related phenomena such as ATP synthesis, ion channel depolarization, and other soft molecular machine quantum effects due to kinetic isotope effects.

Now I'm forgetting where I saw it, but I do remember some link between the parallel beta sheets of NAD+ dependent oxidoreductases, and the perpendicular alpha helices between those parallel beta sheets, and the ability for light to rotate the bond angles of the beta sheets by fixed discrete amounts to form stereoisomers.

From wiki again -- [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Beta\_sheet](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Beta_sheet)

Antiparallel vs parallel beta sheets

### Da-mo — 2014-12-10T04:42:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-10-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12245/post-149646>

While picturing the tilt of axis in the above videos I remembered something about thyristors and firing angles.

> **An organic thyristor**
> Thyristors are solid-state electronic devices that work rather like diodes; once activated a thyristor will remain conducting until the current falls to zero and reverses direction, when it will turn off. They are widely used as inverters (d.c.-to-a.c. converters) and for smooth control of power in a variety of applications, for example motors and refrigerators. Sawano _et al_. now report the discovery of thyristor-like behaviour in a conducting organic salt. Unlike conventional thyristors, this organic material exhibits thyristor-like behaviour as a bulk phenomenon. [http://www.nature.com/nature/journal/v437/n7058/edsumm/e050922-12.html](http://www.nature.com/nature/journal/v437/n7058/edsumm/e050922-12.html)

Just wondering if the controlling signal at the gate is linked to the earth axis tilt in a biological version of the thyristor via angle of incidence of incoming waves. If it is it would help me understand the seasonal impact on circadian biology - and also how location on the surface of the planet attunes metabolism to available food.

### nonchalant — 2014-12-13T02:14:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-10-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12245/post-149968>

> [Da-mo said:](/goto/post?id=149232)
>
> Reflected waves from the shore travelling in the opposite direction causing destructive interference with incoming waves when a trough meets a peak, or constructive interference when a peak meets a peak or when a trough meets a trough.
>
> Another possibility is that there are two different sets of waves travelling in the same direction at different speeds.
>

Last time I was at the beach, it was in a bay. I noticed for a while that the waves came in and went out like normal, but they would break sooner to my right (which was closest to the ocean) and then roll towards me, and finally finish on my left side. I realized that the tide was coming in, which added a force at 90 degrees to the normal wave motion. Hardly mind-blowing, but it is an example of two waves interacting, and I could discern additional information from what I saw. At other times, the waves just came at the normal angle. Also some large ships went by, and of course the waves breaking on the beach got larger for a while, like Jack mentioned.

### nonchalant — 2014-12-13T02:40:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-10-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12245/post-149969>

Generally speaking, large bodies of water seem to reflect what our CSF does. Often at daybreak the water is still and quiet, only to start ruffling in the quickening breeze, which turns into waves during most of the day. Then at sunset the waves often quieten, because the wind dies down. I suppose a lake is usually 'coherent' at night, while during the day it is a jumbled collection of waves from various sources (wind, boats, fish jumping, ducks swimming, birds diving, kids playing...).

I remember as a kid, going to the Gulf beach one day, to find that the constant waves were still, like it was a lake. Quite baffling. The wind was gone, but that usually doesn't make the ocean still. Wonder what would cause that. Doldrums? It's just not the same, walking on the beach with no wave sounds.

### Da-mo — 2014-12-13T08:59:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-10-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12245/post-149987>

I've been fishing near a point in the hook end of a large hook shaped bay where wave energy would kind of get funnelled into where I was. One day it was very flat with only hardly noticeable waves in various directions - but every half hour or so all those myriad little waves would coincide in one spot and the water would just stand up in front of me and then disappear again.

### Jack Kruse — 2016-10-22T02:53:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-10-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12245/post-203174>

Yes — many of you are finally beginning to see the deep isomorphism in Nature correctly here in 2014.

MEMBERS: Remember when I said what we are caliing the Higgs may not be the Higgs at all? The particle was found using mathematic caluculations and not 100% tied to experimental data. So this rasies the question, "MAYBE IT WASN'T THE HIGGS PARTICLE AFTER ALL?" I think when the energies go higher at CERN more pardoxes will be found and not confirmations. I mentioned this in the April 2016 webinar.
Last year CERN announced the finding of a new elementary particle, the Higgs particle. But maybe it wasn't the Higgs particle, maybe it just looks like it. And maybe it is not alone.

Many calculations indicate that the particle discovered last year in the CERN particle accelerator was indeed the famous Higgs particle. Physicists agree that the CERN experiments did find a new particle that had never been seen before, but according to an international research team, there is no conclusive evidence that the particle was indeed the Higgs particle. [http://phys.org/news/2014-11-wasnt-higgs-particle.html](http://phys.org/news/2014-11-wasnt-higgs-particle.html)

### JoeBranca — 2014-12-03T05:09:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rush-or-otherwise-interesting-song-of-day.12246/post-149078>

> [NeilBB said:](/goto/post?id=148979)
>
> Thats a great one too Joe, but perhaps "electricity" should have trumped both "biology" and "chemistry" in the chorus...
>

My introduction to Rush was 1988/89. I was a freshman at a Catholic High School, and I didn't do too well with the attempted forcing of social conformity through dress codes and what not, which did nothing to alleviate the base cruelty of archetypal cliques raging strong (I didn't fit into the right groups). And my older brother, at that time he played for me two vinyl records, Moving Pictures and Signals (these were released during the end of his high school years).

It was a perfect entry point, first the sonic experience of hearing these sounds fresh on vinyl, and second that my bro took the time to walk me through the themes and meaning of every song. Talk about an active listening experience! The lyrics on many songs from those two albums put a little cohesion to that strange point in my young life. And the music _instrumentally_ spoke quite clearly of individual freedom over against arbitrary conventions.

After that initial exposure, I went out with my allowance and bought two cassettes from Tower Records, purely based on which album covers spoke to me musically, which were the blue owl emerging from the snow (Fly By Night) and three crimson spheres suspended in space (Hold Your Fire). It took a while to wrap my head around this being the same band, but I was immersed in each album's own world.

>
> My introduction to Rush was 1988/89. I was a freshman at a Catholic High School, and I didn't do too well with the attempted forcing of social conformity through dress codes and what not, which did nothing to alleviate the base cruelty of archetypal cliques raging strong (I didn't fit into the right groups). And my older brother, at that time he played for me two vinyl records, Moving Pictures and Signals (these were released during the end of his high school years).
>
> It was a perfect entry point, first the sonic experience of hearing these sounds fresh on vinyl, and second that my bro took the time to walk me through the themes and meaning of every song. Talk about an active listening experience! The lyrics on many songs from those two albums put a little cohesion to that strange point in my young life. And the music _instrumentally_ spoke quite clearly of individual freedom over against arbitrary conventions.
>
> After that initial exposure, I went out with my allowance and bought two cassettes from Tower Records, purely based on which album covers spoke to me musically, which were the blue owl emerging from the snow (Fly By Night) and three crimson spheres suspended in space (Hold Your Fire). It took a while to wrap my head around this being the same band, but I was immersed in each album's own world.

I love hearing stories about how people come to music and how it resonates with them. I find it interesting how with both album covers and book jackets that the covers themselves can persuade us to buy things we wouldn't have considered before seeing it. Those covers and the music/words have a resounding impact upon us. I remember first coming upon Sommerset Maughanm's _A Razors Edge_ and was COMPELLED to buy it. I read it in under 3hrs cover to cover. I also remember stumbling upon Talking Heads Little Creatures Album. I was 11 at the time, and it stayed in my "knock off" Walkman. The kids at school made fun of me for listening to crap as they called it... but damn that Album sent me on a spiral into Alternative/Modern Rock before it was cool. shortly there after I picked up the Violent Femmes Add it Up and I didn't look back. I remember when I about 7 I'd take out my mom's HAIR album from the musical - it was AWESOME. And I still have the Don McLean's American Pie Vinyl. The song that struck a chord with me was Vincent - and from there I dived in to the great artists..... its all connected... most don't see it...

### NeilBB — 2014-12-11T20:49:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rush-or-otherwise-interesting-song-of-day.12246/post-149844>

> [Shijin13 said:](/goto/post?id=149820)
>
> Damn @[NeilBB](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/6879/) you're bringing back my childhood.....
>
> when you listen to this song.... and apply the lyrics to what Jack's teaching us.... profound...

Here's the thing. Really good artists and musicians have a way of conveying what's really right and really wrong out there amazingly well a lot of the time, if you just look for it. They don't generally do it with facts and studies, of course. It's more of a right-brain approach, and its abstract, but it can be just as powerful if you are really in tune to it. Art has always been an instrument of social criticism and cultural change.

I've been posting all this stuff not just cause I like the music, but also because I think it's actually quite important.

Did you ever see the Bring on the Night doc, recording the lead up to his solo debut performance? I watched it over and over in high school with my "garage band" buddies, and it filled us with inspiration on the risks of stepping out, defying expectations, following the music you believe is important and not what will be most successful.

### NeilBB — 2014-12-12T17:42:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rush-or-otherwise-interesting-song-of-day.12246/post-149908>

In my opinion outcomes in life are neither totally random, nor totally controllable. Religious types think they have no control, so they sit around and pray all the time. Obsessive types try to control _everything_ and are frustrated when bad luck thwarts them.

In truth, life is exactly like poker, a mixture of skill and luck. Good players win much more consistently than bad players. But on any given day you can draw a bad card and get busted, even when you do everything right. That's just life. In poker, when you do everything correctly and lose anyway, it's called a bad beat.

But there is another saying in poker tournament play. "All you need is a chip and a chair..." Translation: no matter how unlucky you've been in life/health, if you're not dead, you have the potential to improve significantly! But never any guarantees, of course. What other option is there but to try to win?

[http://m.lvsun.com/news/2009/nov/11/chip-and-chair-axiom-proven-again/](http://m.lvsun.com/news/2009/nov/11/chip-and-chair-axiom-proven-again/)

>
> In truth, life is exactly like poker, a mixture of skill and luck. Good players win much more consistently than bad players. But on any given day you can draw a bad card and get busted, even when you do everything right. That's just life. In poker, when you do everything correctly and lose anyway, it's called a bad beat.
>
> But there is another saying in poker tournament play. "All you need is a chip and a chair..." Translation: no matter how unlucky you've been in life/health, if you're not dead, you have the potential to improve significantly! But never any guarantees, of course. What other option is there but to try to win?
>
>
>
> [http://m.lvsun.com/news/2009/nov/11/chip-and-chair-axiom-proven-again/](http://m.lvsun.com/news/2009/nov/11/chip-and-chair-axiom-proven-again/)

Well said @[NeilBB](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/6879/) Love the poker analogy. when you get knocked down you've got to get back up otherwise you're roadkill. As I watch people struggle here on the forum.... the Knee Deep by Zac Brown & Jimmy Buffet keeps popping into my mind...

sometimes you've got to be willing to jump, change the environment you find yourself in, to help stack the deck in your favor. as a friend who has an amazing faith in God told me, sometimes you've gotta jump with out the parachute or the net and trust that you'll land on both feet. I think for many people (those here on the forum and those in my daily life) that's the hardest thing to embrace. trusting the Universe you're connected too will take care of all you need if you just follow your true north (that voice inside your head that's telling you you've gotta make the change even if you don't know where the change is going to take you).

here's the song Knee Deep

and lyrics [http://www.azlyrics.com/lyrics/zacbrownband/kneedeep.html](http://www.azlyrics.com/lyrics/zacbrownband/kneedeep.html)
